A unit mismatch alone can cost **3–4 marks** out of 7. Always write: $G_L$ in kg/(m²·s) when $\dot{m}_s$ is in kg/s, OR $G_L$ in kg/(m²·h) when $\dot{m}_s$ is in kg/h. **Do not mix them**.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Describe the Coe–Clevenger method for the determination of thickener area. How is it different from the Kynch method?**

**Model Answer**:

**Coe–Clevenger Method (4 marks)**:

1. Conduct **multiple** batch settling tests on the slurry at *different* initial concentrations spanning the expected operating range.
2. For each test, record the interface height $H$ vs. time $t$ and the final sludge height $H_u$.
3. For each test, the solids flux at concentration $C$ is computed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G(C) = \frac{H_0 - H(t)}{t \cdot \left( \dfrac{1}{C} - \dfrac{1}{C_u} \right)^{-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

4. The required thickener area for each test is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A(C) = \frac{V (C_0 - C_u)}{C_u \cdot G(C) \cdot t}
\end{aligned}
$$

5. The maximum $A(C)$ across all tests is the design area.

**Difference from Kynch method (3 marks)**:

| Feature | Coe–Clevenger | Kynch |
|---|---|---|
| Number of batch tests | Multiple (one per concentration) | **Single** test suffices |
| Flux determination | Direct from $H(t)$ slope and concentration relation | Graphical tangent construction |
| Data requirement | More extensive | Minimal — interface height vs. time only |
| Speed | Slow and laborious | **Quick** and elegant |
| Year introduced | 1916 | 1952 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Kynch's 1952 method is now preferred** in industry and academics because it extracts the entire flux curve from *one* settling test via the elegant tangent construction, whereas Coe–Clevenger required several tests spanning different concentrations.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A 1-litre cylinder test on a lime-mud slurry (initial height 35 cm, $\rho_s = 2200$ kg/m³, $\rho_l = 1100$ kg/m³, $C_0 = 90$ kg/m³) gives the following data. Determine the limiting solid flux and the minimum thickener area for a feed rate of 25 m³/h.**

| $t$ (s) | 0 | 60 | 120 | 300 | 600 | 900 | 1800 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $H$ (m) | 0.35 | 0.32 | 0.295 | 0.255 | 0.215 | 0.190 | 0.165 |

**Model Answer**:

**Step 1 — Locate the compression point (1 mark)**:
The slope $\Delta H/\Delta t$ between consecutive points:
- 0–60 s: $5.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s
- 60–120 s: $4.17 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s
- 120–300 s: $2.22 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s
- 300–600 s: $1.33 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s
- 600–900 s: $8.33 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s
- 900–1800 s: $2.78 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s

The slope drops sharply after 600 s — the **compression point is at $t = 600$ s, $H = 0.215$ m**.

**Step 2 — Settling velocity at compression point (1.5 marks)**:
Use a tangent through (600, 0.215) and (1800, 0.165):

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_L &= -\frac{0.165 - 0.215}{1800 - 600} = \frac{0.050}{1200} \\
u_L &= 4.17 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Concentration at compression point (1 mark)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_L &= C_0 \cdot \frac{H_0}{H_L} = 90 \cdot \frac{0.35}{0.215} = 146.5 \text{ kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Limiting flux (1 mark)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_L &= C_L \cdot u_L = 146.5 \times 4.17 \times 10^{-5} \\
G_L &= 6.11 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)} = 22.0 \text{ kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{h)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Feed mass rate (1 mark)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_s &= F \cdot C_0 = \frac{25 \times 90}{3600} = 0.625 \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Thickener area (1 mark)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\dot{m}_s}{G_L} = \frac{0.625}{6.11 \times 10^{-3}} = 102.3 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Equivalent diameter (0.5 marks)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \sqrt{\frac{4A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 102.3}{\pi}} \approx 11.4 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**:
> 1. **Identifying the compression point** correctly is the trickiest part — it requires reading the *kink* in the $H$–$t$ curve. Picking an arbitrary point loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the negative sign** in $u_L = -dH/dt$ gives a wrong (negative) flux — a hard-to-spot mistake worth 1 mark.
> 3. **Using $C_0$ in the area formula** instead of $C_L$ in the flux calculation is the most common error; remember $G = C \cdot u$ uses the *local* concentration, not the feed concentration.
> 4. **Not converting units** (m³/h to m³/s) accounts for ~30% of valuation losses in this experiment.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- [ ] **Sedimentation** = gravity-based solid–liquid separation; the practical lab operation is the *batch cylinder test* used to extract settling data.
- [ ] **Four zones** form during batch settling: **A** (clear liquid) → **B** (hindered settling, constant C) → **C** (transition) → **D** (compression).
- [ ] **Mass conservation**: $C(t) \cdot H(t) = C_0 \cdot H_0$ — the *single most useful relation* in this experiment.
- [ ] **Stokes' law** is the canonical terminal velocity equation; remember the $1/18$ factor and the validity range $Re_p < 0.2$.
- [ ] **Solid flux** $G = C \cdot u_s$ (kg/m²·s) — peaks at the **limiting flux** $G_L$ corresponding to the **compression point**.
- [ ] **Kynch method** uses a *single* batch test; the tangent at the compression point directly gives $G_L$.
- [ ] **Coe–Clevenger method** uses *multiple* batch tests at different concentrations; it is laborious but historically significant.
- [ ] **Thickener area formula**: $A = \dot{m}_s / G_L$ — the *design equation*. **Watch the units**.
- [ ] **Equivalent diameter**: $D = \sqrt{4A/\pi}$.
- [ ] **Thickener is area-limited, not volume-limited** — depth is set by residence time required for compression consolidation.
- [ ] **Rake** aids thickening by: (i) moving sludge to discharge, (ii) breaking flocs, (iii) preventing channels.
- [ ] **Feed-well design** matters: feed must enter *below* the surface to prevent short-circuiting and surface disturbances.
- [ ] **Operating below $G_L$** is mandatory; choking the thickener causes cloudy overflow and process upset.
- [ ] **Re-check $C_0$ calculation** when slurry is given in *mass %* — convert via $\rho_s$ and $\rho_l$ to get kg/m³.
- [ ] **KTU 2024 viva favourites**: Why hindered settling is slower than free settling; meaning of compression point; why thickener is shallow and wide; role of rake; difference between clarifier and thickener; why Stokes' law fails above $Re_p = 0.2$.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for $A = \dot{m}_s / G_L$**:
> *"**Area = Mass flow divided by Limiting flux**. The thickener must be big enough in cross-section to 'swallow' the incoming mass of solids; if you shrink the area, the solids have no place to land and they pile up, choking the unit."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Verification of the laws of crushing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL407 - PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Verification of the laws of crushing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Three Fundamental Laws of Crushing

The mechanical breakdown of solid particles into smaller fragments consumes energy, and the relationship between the **energy supplied** and the **size reduction achieved** is described by three classical laws. These are universally tested in the **Jaw Crusher / Roll Crusher experiment** under the KTU PCCHL407 syllabus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definitions (KTU Board Standard Wording)**
>
> **1. Kick's Law (1885):** *The energy required for crushing a given quantity of material is directly proportional to the logarithm of the ratio of the initial and final particle sizes.*
>
> **2. Rittinger's Law (1867):** *The energy required for crushing is directly proportional to the new surface area produced, i.e., to the difference between the reciprocals of the product and feed sizes.*
>
> **3. Bond's Law (1952):** *The energy required for crushing is proportional to the square root of the surface-to-volume ratio, expressed as the difference between the reciprocals of the square roots of product and feed sizes.*

| Law | Best Suited For | Energy Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| Kick's Law | Coarse crushing (large particles) | **Volume deformation** |
| Rittinger's Law | Fine grinding (small particles) | **New surface creation** |
| Bond's Law | Intermediate crushing (industrial standard) | **Crack propagation** |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **"Breaking a Coconut" Analogy**
> Imagine you must break a **large coconut** into powder.
> - The **first hammer blow** (Kick's regime) cracks it with relatively *little* energy compared to the size change — because most of the energy goes into deforming the bulk volume.
> - The **next few blows** that turn the cracked pieces into **coarse grit** follow **Bond's Law** — moderate energy, the cracks are propagating through flaws.
> - The **final, prolonged grinding** with a stone pestle that converts grit into fine powder follows **Rittinger's Law** — every tiny particle needs a *new surface* to be created, demanding exponentially more energy.
>
> This is precisely why industrial mills consume *huge* amounts of power in the final fine-grinding stage!

## 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics Used

- **Feed size (F)** and **Product size (P)** are expressed as the **arithmetic, geometric, harmonic, or surface mean diameter** (in **mm** or **µm**).
- **Specific energy consumption (E)** is expressed as **kWh/tonne** of material crushed.
- The **Work Index (Wᵢ)** of a material is defined as the energy (in **kWh**) required to reduce **one short ton** (≈ **907.185 kg**) of material from an infinite feed size to **80% passing 100 µm** — a benchmark of grindability.
- Standard test sieve sizes used: **4.75 mm, 2.36 mm, 1.18 mm, 600 µm, 425 µm, 300 µm, 212 µm, 150 µm, 75 µm** (per **IS 460** and **ASTM E11**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Kick's Law — Detailed Logical Breakdown

**Statement:** Kick proposed that the energy required for size reduction is proportional to the **logarithmic reduction ratio**.

**Why it works (physical reasoning):** Kick assumed that particles fracture by **volume distortion** (like squeezing a sponge). The strain energy stored is proportional to the *log* of the deformation ratio. For a particle of size `D`, the strain is roughly `ln(F/P)`, hence the energy law.

- The reduction ratio `R = F / P` must be **dimensionless** and `> 1`.
- Kick's Law is the most accurate for **coarse crushing** (rod mills, jaw crushers in primary stage).

## 2.2 Rittinger's Law — Detailed Logical Breakdown

**Statement:** Energy is proportional to **new surface area created** during comminution.

**Why it works (physical reasoning):** Rittinger reasoned that each fracture creates **two new surfaces** (in addition to the original). If a particle of diameter `D` breaks into `n` smaller particles of diameter `d`, the increase in surface area is `n · π·d² − π·D²`. Since `n·d³ ≈ D³` (volume conservation, with constant density `ρ`), the new surface area per unit mass becomes `K·(1/d − 1/D)`.

- Highly accurate for **fine grinding** (ball mills in the < 200 µm range).
- Fails for coarse particles because the surface area change is then negligible.

## 2.3 Bond's Law — Detailed Logical Breakdown

**Statement:** A compromise between the volume and surface theories; energy is proportional to the **square root of the surface-to-volume ratio**, formalized through the **Work Index (Wᵢ)**.

**Why it works (physical reasoning):** Bond observed that industrial crushing involves *crack propagation* through microscopic flaws (Griffith's theory). The energy required to extend a crack scales with `√(length)`, leading to a `1/√D` dependence.

- **Bond's Law is the most widely used in industry** because it covers the full range from coarse to fine.
- The **Work Index `Wᵢ`** is material-specific (e.g., limestone ≈ 12.74 kWh/tonne, quartzite ≈ 12.18 kWh/tonne, granite ≈ 15.13 kWh/tonne).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Law | Equation (per unit mass) | Units of Constant `K` | Best Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Kick's Law** | $E = K_K \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{F}{P}\right)$ | kWh/tonne | Coarse crushing |
| 2 | **Rittinger's Law** | $E = K_R \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{P} - \dfrac{1}{F}\right)$ | kWh·mm/tonne | Fine grinding |
| 3 | **Bond's Law** | $E = W_i \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{F}}\right)$ | kWh/tonne | Industrial standard |

**Mean Diameter Calculation Formulas (used to obtain F and P):**

| # | Mean Type | Formula | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Arithmetic Mean** | $\bar{d} = \dfrac{\sum (x_i \cdot d_i)}{\sum x_i}$ | Quick check |
| 2 | **Geometric Mean** | $\log \bar{d} = \dfrac{\sum [x_i \cdot \log d_i]}{\sum x_i}$ | Wide distributions |
| 3 | **Harmonic Mean** | $\dfrac{1}{\bar{d}} = \dfrac{\sum \left(\dfrac{x_i}{d_i}\right)}{\sum x_i}$ | Surface-related laws |
| 4 | **Surface Mean (Sauter)** | $\bar{d}_S = \dfrac{\sum x_i}{\sum \left(\dfrac{x_i}{d_i}\right)}$ | Rittinger's Law verification |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility Note:** These three laws are the foundation of **comminution circuit design** in cement plants, mineral processing (copper, iron ore), pharmaceutical powder production, and food processing (sugar, spice grinding). Selecting the *wrong law* leads to over-sized mills, wasted energy (≈ 1–2 % of global electricity), and incorrect scale-up.

## 2.5 Verification Strategy (Experimental Logic)

1. Feed a known mass `M` of material into the **jaw crusher**.
2. Record electrical energy `E_total` consumed (voltmeter × ammeter × time) → specific energy `E = E_total / M`.
3. Perform **sieve analysis** on feed and product → calculate `F` and `P`.
4. Compute three expressions: `ln(F/P)`, `(1/P − 1/F)`, `(1/√P − 1/√F)`.
5. The law that produces a **constant ratio** `E / expression = K` for all runs is the **verified law** for that material and size range.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Rittinger's Law (Full Rigor)

**Assumption (KTU standard):** All the energy supplied during comminution is consumed in creating **new surface area**, and no energy is lost as heat, sound, or plastic deformation.

**Step 1 — Surface area of a single particle:**
A spherical particle of diameter `D` has surface area:
$$A_{sphere} = \pi \cdot D^2$$

**Step 2 — Surface area per unit mass:**
If the particle density is `ρ_s`, the mass of one particle is:
$$m_{particle} = \frac{\pi}{6} \cdot D^3 \cdot \rho_s$$
Hence the **specific surface area** (per unit mass) is:
$$\frac{A}{m} = \frac{\pi \cdot D^2}{\frac{\pi}{6} \cdot D^3 \cdot \rho_s} = \frac{6}{\rho_s \cdot D}$$

**Step 3 — New surface area created:**
A feed particle of size `D` (mass `m_F`) breaks into `n` product particles of size `d` (mass `m_P`). By conservation of mass:
$$m_F = n \cdot m_P \quad \Rightarrow \quad n = \frac{D^3}{d^3}$$

New surface area (ignoring original surface as negligible when `d << D`):
$$A_{new} = n \cdot \pi d^2 = \frac{D^3}{d^3} \cdot \pi d^2 = \frac{\pi \cdot D^3}{d}$$

Per unit mass, the new specific surface area is:
$$\Delta S = \frac{A_{new}}{m_F} = \frac{\pi \cdot D^3 / d}{\frac{\pi}{6} \cdot D^3 \cdot \rho_s} = \frac{6}{\rho_s \cdot d}$$

**Step 4 — Energy proportionality:**
Since energy `E` is proportional to the new specific surface:
$$E = K_R \cdot \left( \frac{1}{d_{product}} - \frac{1}{d_{feed}} \right) = K_R \cdot \left( \frac{1}{P} - \frac{1}{F} \right)$$

The constant `K_R = 6 / \rho_s` only if 100 % of energy becomes surface energy — in practice, `K_R` is determined **experimentally** because real crushing is far less efficient (≈ 1 % conversion).

## 3.2 Derivation of Kick's Law (KTU Derivation Pattern)

**Step 1:** Let the energy for an *infinitesimal* size reduction `dD` be proportional to the volume, hence:
$$dE = -K_K \cdot \frac{dD}{D}$$
The negative sign indicates energy is *consumed* as size `D` decreases.

**Step 2:** Integrate from feed size `F` to product size `P`:
$$\int_{0}^{E} dE = -K_K \int_{F}^{P} \frac{dD}{D}$$

$$E = -K_K \cdot [\ln D]_{F}^{P} = -K_K \cdot (\ln P - \ln F)$$

$$E = K_K \cdot \ln\left(\frac{F}{P}\right)$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Bond's Law (Third-Power Compromise)

**Step 1:** Bond postulated that the energy for an *infinitesimal* reduction scales with the square root of the particle size:
$$dE = -K_B \cdot \frac{dD}{D^{1.5}}$$

**Step 2:** Integrate from `F` to `P`:
$$\int_{0}^{E} dE = -K_B \int_{F}^{P} D^{-1.5}\, dD$$

$$E = -K_B \left[ \frac{D^{-0.5}}{-0.5} \right]_{F}^{P} = 2K_B \left[ \frac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F}} \right]$$

Defining `W_i = 2K_B`:
$$E = W_i \cdot \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F}} \right)$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Full KTU Pattern)

**Problem Statement (Typical Lab Data):**
A jaw crusher takes **2.5 kg of limestone** feed. The voltmeter reads **220 V**, the ammeter reads **4.5 A**, and the crusher runs for **30 seconds**. The mean feed size is **5.0 mm** and the mean product size is **1.2 mm**. Verify which law of crushing is obeyed by this data.

**Step A — Specific Energy Calculation:**
$$E_{total} = V \times I \times t = 220 \times 4.5 \times 30 = 29{,}700 \text{ J}$$

$$E_{specific} = \frac{29{,}700}{2.5} = 11{,}880 \text{ J/kg} = \frac{11{,}880}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 3.30 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kWh/kg}$$

Convert to **kWh/tonne** (multiply by 1000):
$$E = 3.30 \text{ kWh/tonne}$$

**Step B — Compute the three "Law Expressions":**

**Kick's expression:**
$$K_{expr} = \ln\left(\frac{5.0}{1.2}\right) = \ln(4.1667) = 1.4271$$

**Rittinger's expression:**
$$R_{expr} = \left(\frac{1}{1.2} - \frac{1}{5.0}\right) = 0.8333 - 0.2000 = 0.6333 \text{ mm}^{-1}$$

**Bond's expression:**
$$B_{expr} = \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1.2}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{5.0}}\right) = 0.9129 - 0.4472 = 0.4657 \text{ mm}^{-0.5}$$

**Step C — Determine the Proportionality Constant `K` for Each Law:**

| Law | Calculation | Value of `K` (constant) |
|---|---|---|
| Kick | $K_K = E / K_{expr} = 3.30 / 1.4271$ | **2.312 kWh/tonne** |
| Rittinger | $K_R = E / R_{expr} = 3.30 / 0.6333$ | **5.211 kWh·mm/tonne** |
| Bond | $W_i = E / B_{expr} = 3.30 / 0.4657$ | **7.087 kWh/tonne** |

**Step D — Verification with a Second Run:**
Suppose a second trial gives `E₂ = 4.50 kWh/tonne`, `F₂ = 5.0 mm`, `P₂ = 0.9 mm`.

| Law | New Expression | New `K` | % Deviation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Kick | $\ln(5.0/0.9) = 1.7148$ | $4.50/1.7148 = 2.624$ | **13.5 %** |
| Rittinger | $(1/0.9 - 1/5.0) = 0.9111$ | $4.50/0.9111 = 4.939$ | **5.2 %** |
| Bond | $(1/\sqrt{0.9} - 1/\sqrt{5.0}) = 0.6072$ | $4.50/0.6072 = 7.411$ | **4.6 %** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The law whose constant `K` remains **nearly unchanged** between runs is the verified one. Here, **Bond's Law** shows the lowest percentage deviation (4.6 %), so the data verifies **Bond's Law** for limestone in this size range.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix (Which Law to Use?)

| Particle Size Range | Dominant Mechanism | Recommended Law |
|---|---|---|
| > 50 mm | Volume deformation | **Kick's Law** |
| 1 – 50 mm | Crack propagation | **Bond's Law** |
| < 1 mm | Surface area creation | **Rittinger's Law** |
| 0.1 – 1 mm | Industrial ball mills | **Bond's Law** (with Wᵢ) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Jaw Crusher Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Weigh Feed Material] --> B[Step 2: Record Initial Sieve Analysis of Feed]
    B --> C[Step 3: Measure V and I of Idle Motor]
    C --> D[Step 4: Feed Material into Jaw Crusher]
    D --> E[Step 5: Run Crusher for Fixed Time t]
    E --> F[Step 6: Collect Crushed Product and Weigh]
    F --> G[Step 7: Record V and I Under Load]
    G --> H[Step 8: Sieve Analysis of Product]
    H --> I[Step 9: Calculate Mean Feed Size F]
    I --> J[Step 10: Calculate Mean Product Size P]
    J --> K[Step 11: Compute Specific Energy E]
    K --> L{Step 12: Test Three Law Expressions}
    L --> M[Evaluate Kick Constant K-K]
    L --> N[Evaluate Rittinger Constant K-R]
    L --> O[Evaluate Bond Constant W-i]
    M --> P[Step 13: Compare Constants Across Runs]
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Step 14: Law with Least Deviation is Verified]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram of the Jaw Crusher Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POWER[Power Supply Module]
        PWR1[220V AC Mains]
        PWR2[Variac Voltage Regulator]
        PWR3[Voltmeter Display]
    end

    subgraph MOTOR[Drive Module]
        M1[3-Phase Induction Motor]
        M2[Ammeter Display]
        M3[Timer Module]
    end

    subgraph CRUSHER[Crushing Module]
        C1[Hopper and Feed Chute]
        C2[Fixed Jaw Plate]
        C3[Moving Jaw Plate]
        C4[Eccentric Shaft Assembly]
        C5[Discharge Port]
    end

    subgraph SIEVE[Analysis Module]
        S1[Ro-Tap Sieve Shaker]
        S2[Stacked Standard Sieves]
        S3[Electronic Balance]
    end

    PWR1 --> PWR2 --> PWR3
    PWR2 --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    M1 --> M3
    M1 --> C4
    C1 --> C2
    C4 --> C3
    C2 --> C5
    C3 --> C5
    C5 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
```

## 4.3 Energy Transformation Flow During Crushing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ELEC[Electrical Energy Input E-in] --> MOT[Mechanical Energy at Motor Shaft]
    MOT --> FRIC[Friction Losses in Bearings]
    MOT --> CRUSH[Effective Crushing Energy E-effective]
    FRIC --> HEAT1[Heat Loss to Environment]
    CRUSH --> NEW[New Surface Area Creation]
    CRUSH --> CRACK[Crack Propagation Energy]
    CRUSH --> SOUND[Acoustic and Vibration Loss]
    CRUSH --> DEFORM[Plastic Deformation of Particles]
    NEW --> PRODS[Product Surface Energy]
    CRACK --> HEAT2[Heat of Fracture]
```

## 4.4 Sieve Analysis Data Reduction Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sample Weighed Mass M] --> B[Sieve Through Nested Sieves]
    B --> C[Mass Retained on Each Sieve m-i]
    C --> D[Calculate Mass Fraction x-i equals m-i divided by M]
    D --> E[Tabulate d-i as Geometric Mean of Adjacent Sieve Openings]
    E --> F[Compute Arithmetic Mean Diameter F or P]
    E --> G[Compute Harmonic Mean for Rittinger]
    E --> H[Compute Surface Mean for Bond]
    F --> I[Use in Law Equations]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Verify Law by Constancy of K]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCCHL407]`
**State Rittinger's Law of crushing and mention the condition under which it is most accurate.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Rittinger's Law (1867):** The energy required for crushing a solid substance is **directly proportional to the new surface area produced** during size reduction. Mathematically:
>
> $$E = K_R \cdot \left( \frac{1}{P} - \frac{1}{F} \right)$$
>
> where `F` = feed size, `P` = product size, `K_R` = Rittinger's constant.
>
> **Condition of accuracy:** Rittinger's Law is most accurate in the **fine grinding range** (particle sizes **below 1 mm**), where the creation of new surface area dominates the energy consumption. It is **not** suitable for coarse crushing because the surface area change is then negligible.

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PCCHL407]`
**Define the term 'Work Index' (Wᵢ) as used in Bond's Law of crushing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **Work Index (Wᵢ)** of a material is defined as the **energy required in kWh to reduce one short ton (≈ 907.185 kg)** of the material from a theoretical infinite feed size down to a size such that **80 % of the product passes through a 100 µm sieve (i.e., P₈₀ = 100 µm)**.
>
> $$W_i = \frac{E}{\left( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{F_{80}}} \right)}$$
>
> It is a **material-specific grindability parameter** used in the design and scale-up of industrial crushing and grinding equipment. Typical values: limestone ≈ 12.74 kWh/tonne, granite ≈ 15.13 kWh/tonne.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PCCHL407]`

**(a)** Derive Rittinger's Law of crushing starting from the assumption that the energy supplied is entirely used in creating new surface area. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In an experiment to verify the laws of crushing using a jaw crusher, the following data was recorded:

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Mass of feed | 3.0 kg |
| Voltage (V) | 230 V |
| Current under load (I) | 5.2 A |
| Run time (t) | 25 s |
| Mean feed size (F) | 4.5 mm |
| Mean product size (P) | 1.0 mm |

Calculate the constants for Kick's, Rittinger's, and Bond's laws. A second run with the same feed size but a product size of 0.8 mm gives a specific energy of 4.8 kWh/tonne. Identify which law is best verified. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of Rittinger's Law:**

*Assumption 1:* The feed particles are assumed to be **spherical** with diameter `D`.
*Assumption 2:* All the energy supplied is **completely utilized in producing new surface area** (no losses to heat, sound, or vibration).
*Assumption 3:* Particle **density ρ_s remains constant** during crushing (incompressible solid).
*Assumption 4:* The original surface area of the feed particle is **negligible** compared to the new surface area of the product particles.

*Derivation:*

Surface area of one feed particle of diameter `D`:
$$A_F = \pi D^2$$

Surface area of one product particle of diameter `d`:
$$A_P = \pi d^2$$

If one feed particle breaks into `n` product particles, **mass conservation** gives:
$$\frac{\pi}{6} D^3 \rho_s = n \cdot \frac{\pi}{6} d^3 \rho_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad n = \left(\frac{D}{d}\right)^3$$

Total new surface area (after neglecting original):
$$A_{new} = n \cdot \pi d^2 = \left(\frac{D}{d}\right)^3 \cdot \pi d^2 = \frac{\pi D^3}{d}$$

Per unit mass of material, the new specific surface area is:
$$\Delta S = \frac{A_{new}}{m} = \frac{\pi D^3 / d}{\frac{\pi}{6} D^3 \rho_s} = \frac{6}{\rho_s \, d}$$

Since `F` and `P` are the feed and product mean diameters, **specific energy** is proportional to the new specific surface:
$$E = K_R \cdot \left(\frac{1}{P} - \frac{1}{F}\right)$$

where `K_R` is Rittinger's constant determined experimentally (its theoretical limit is `6/ρ_s`, but real systems are far less efficient).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Verification:**

*Run 1:*

Specific energy calculation:
$$E_1 = \frac{V \times I \times t}{M} = \frac{230 \times 5.2 \times 25}{3.0} = 9966.67 \text{ J/kg}$$

Converting to kWh/tonne:
$$E_1 = \frac{9966.67}{3.6 \times 10^6} \times 1000 = 2.7685 \text{ kWh/tonne}$$

Compute the three law expressions:

| Law | Expression | Numerical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Kick | $\ln(F/P) = \ln(4.5/1.0)$ | $1.5041$ |
| Rittinger | $1/P - 1/F = 1.0 - 0.2222$ | $0.7778 \text{ mm}^{-1}$ |
| Bond | $1/\sqrt{P} - 1/\sqrt{F} = 1.0 - 0.4714$ | $0.5286 \text{ mm}^{-0.5}$ |

Calculate the constants for Run 1:

| Law | Constant K₁ | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Kick | $K_K = 2.7685 / 1.5041$ | **1.8408 kWh/tonne** |
| Rittinger | $K_R = 2.7685 / 0.7778$ | **3.5594 kWh·mm/tonne** |
| Bond | $W_i = 2.7685 / 0.5286$ | **5.2373 kWh/tonne** |

*Run 2:* `E₂ = 4.8 kWh/tonne`, `F₂ = 4.5 mm`, `P₂ = 0.8 mm`

| Law | New Expression | New Constant K₂ | % Deviation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Kick | $\ln(4.5/0.8) = 1.7251$ | $4.8 / 1.7251 = 2.7826$ | **51.2 %** |
| Rittinger | $1/0.8 - 1/4.5 = 1.0278$ | $4.8 / 1.0278 = 4.6706$ | **31.2 %** |
| Bond | $1/\sqrt{0.8} - 1/\sqrt{4.5} = 0.6495$ | $4.8 / 0.6495 = 7.3903$ | **41.1 %** |

> [!NOTE]
> The percentage deviations are all substantial because the second run was performed on a different product size. However, the law that would give the *most consistent* K when the *same* product size is reproduced is the verified one. In an actual experiment, **Run 1 and Run 2 are replicates** with identical F and P to test repeatability.

**Conclusion:** For a controlled experiment with replicates, **Bond's Law** typically gives the most consistent constant for medium-size crushing in a jaw crusher and is therefore the most commonly verified law in the lab.

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCCHL407]`

**(a)** Explain the three laws of crushing — Kick's, Rittinger's, and Bond's — with their mathematical expressions. Discuss the range of particle sizes for which each law is most suitable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The sieve analysis of feed and product from a jaw crusher experiment is tabulated below:

| Sieve No. | Sieve Size (mm) | Feed Retained (g) | Product Retained (g) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4.750 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 2.360 | 150 | 0 |
| 3 | 1.180 | 200 | 50 |
| 4 | 0.600 | 250 | 150 |
| 5 | 0.425 | 200 | 200 |
| 6 | 0.300 | 100 | 200 |
| 7 | 0.212 | 50 | 150 |
| 8 | 0.150 | 30 | 100 |
| 9 | 0.075 | 15 | 80 |
| Pan | — | 5 | 70 |

The total energy consumed was **0.012 kWh** for a **4 kg** sample. Calculate the harmonic mean feed size `F` and product size `P`, and verify Rittinger's Law. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Three Laws Comparison (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Kick's Law | Rittinger's Law | Bond's Law |
|---|---|---|---|
| Year | 1885 | 1867 | 1952 |
| Equation | $E = K_K \ln(F/P)$ | $E = K_R(1/P - 1/F)$ | $E = W_i(1/\sqrt{P} - 1/\sqrt{F})$ |
| Physical basis | Volume deformation | New surface creation | Crack propagation |
| Particle range | **> 50 mm (coarse)** | **< 1 mm (fine)** | **1–50 mm (intermediate)** |
| Industrial use | Jaw crusher primary | Ball mill fine | Rod/Ball mill, scale-up |
| Limitation | Ignores surface change | Ignores bulk deformation | Most balanced; uses Wᵢ |

---

**Part (b) — Harmonic Mean Calculation:**

For harmonic mean, we use the formula:
$$\frac{1}{\bar{d}} = \frac{\sum (x_i / d_i)}{\sum x_i}$$

First compute mass fractions (using total feed = 1000 g, product = 1000 g):

**Feed harmonic mean:**

| Sieve (mm) | $d_i$ (mm) | $m_i$ (g) | $x_i = m_i/1000$ | $x_i / d_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2.360 | 2.360 | 150 | 0.150 | 0.06356 |
| 1.180 | 1.180 | 200 | 0.200 | 0.16949 |
| 0.600 | 0.600 | 250 | 0.250 | 0.41667 |
| 0.425 | 0.425 | 200 | 0.200 | 0.47059 |
| 0.300 | 0.300 | 100 | 0.100 | 0.33333 |
| 0.212 | 0.212 | 50 | 0.050 | 0.23585 |
| 0.150 | 0.150 | 30 | 0.030 | 0.20000 |
| 0.075 | 0.075 | 15 | 0.015 | 0.20000 |
| Pan (assume 0.038) | 0.038 | 5 | 0.005 | 0.13158 |
| **Sum** | — | **1000** | **1.000** | **2.22107** |

$$F = \frac{1}{2.22107} = 0.4502 \text{ mm}$$

**Product harmonic mean:**

| Sieve (mm) | $d_i$ (mm) | $m_i$ (g) | $x_i$ | $x_i / d_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1.180 | 1.180 | 50 | 0.050 | 0.04237 |
| 0.600 | 0.600 | 150 | 0.150 | 0.25000 |
| 0.425 | 0.425 | 200 | 0.200 | 0.47059 |
| 0.300 | 0.300 | 200 | 0.200 | 0.66667 |
| 0.212 | 0.212 | 150 | 0.150 | 0.70755 |
| 0.150 | 0.150 | 100 | 0.100 | 0.66667 |
| 0.075 | 0.075 | 80 | 0.080 | 1.06667 |
| Pan (0.038) | 0.038 | 70 | 0.070 | 1.84211 |
| **Sum** | — | **1000** | **1.000** | **5.71263** |

$$P = \frac{1}{5.71263} = 0.1750 \text{ mm}$$

**Verification of Rittinger's Law:**

Specific energy consumed:
$$E = \frac{0.012 \text{ kWh}}{4 \text{ kg}} = 0.003 \text{ kWh/kg} = 3.0 \text{ kWh/tonne}$$

Rittinger expression:
$$R_{expr} = \frac{1}{P} - \frac{1}{F} = \frac{1}{0.1750} - \frac{1}{0.4502} = 5.7143 - 2.2212 = 3.4931 \text{ mm}^{-1}$$

Rittinger's constant:
$$K_R = \frac{E}{R_{expr}} = \frac{3.0}{3.4931} = 0.8588 \text{ kWh·mm/tonne}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Rittinger's constant `K_R = 0.8588 kWh·mm/tonne` is calculated. To *verify* the law, a second experiment with the same material and a *different* product size `P'` should yield a similar `K_R`. If `K_R` remains constant (within ± 5 %), Rittinger's Law is verified. The harmonic mean is used here because it is the most surface-sensitive mean and is theoretically consistent with Rittinger's surface-area postulate.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (Read Carefully!)**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency in Energy:** Students often compute energy in **J/kg** but compare it with the Rittinger expression in **mm⁻¹** without converting to consistent units. Always convert to **kWh/tonne** (or at least state the units of `K` explicitly).
>
> 2. **Using the Wrong Mean Diameter:** Using **arithmetic mean** for Rittinger's Law will give an **inconsistent constant** because Rittinger's Law is fundamentally about *surface area*. Always use **harmonic mean** (or **surface mean**) for Rittinger/Bond verifications.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to Neglect Pan Content:** If a significant mass collects in the pan, students often assign `d = 0`, causing division-by-zero. Use **d = (smallest sieve)/2** for the pan fraction as a convention.
>
> 4. **Idle Power Subtraction:** Many students calculate energy from `V × I × t` *without subtracting* the **idle no-load power** of the crusher motor. The correct specific energy is:
>    `E = (V × I_load × t_load − V × I_idle × t_idle) / M`
>
> 5. **Confusing 'F' and 'P':** `F` (feed) is always the **larger** size, `P` (product) is the **smaller** size. Swapping them gives a negative logarithm and an absurd result.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the 'Work Index' Units:** In Bond's Law, the units of `W_i` are **kWh/tonne** (or **kWh/short ton** in US customary). Stating `W_i = 12.74` *without units* will lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Memorize Before Exam**
>
> - **Three laws:** Kick (volume), Rittinger (surface), Bond (crack propagation) — each valid in a specific size range.
> - **Kick's equation:** $E = K_K \cdot \ln(F/P)$ — best for **coarse crushing > 50 mm**.
> - **Rittinger's equation:** $E = K_R \cdot (1/P - 1/F)$ — best for **fine grinding < 1 mm**.
> - **Bond's equation:** $E = W_i \cdot (1/\sqrt{P} - 1/\sqrt{F})$ — **industrial standard**, uses **Work Index**.
> - **Work Index Wᵢ** = energy (kWh) per short ton to reduce infinite feed to 80 % passing 100 µm.
> - **F > P** always; if not, re-check your sieve data.
> - **Use harmonic mean** for Rittinger/Bond verifications; **arithmetic mean** is only for quick sanity checks.
> - **Pan fraction diameter** is taken as **half of the smallest sieve** opening.
> - **Subtract idle power** before computing specific energy.
> - **Mermaid diagrams** in the experiment: Hopper → Jaw → Discharge → Sieves → Balance.
> - **Verification test:** Run the experiment twice (or more); the law whose `K` varies the **least** is verified.
> - **Typical Wᵢ values:** Limestone ≈ 12.74, Granite ≈ 15.13, Quartzite ≈ 12.18, Dolomite ≈ 11.27 (all in kWh/short ton).
> - **Energy units conversion:** $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J} = 860 \text{ kcal}$.
> - **Real grinding efficiency is ≈ 1 %** — most energy is wasted as heat, sound, and friction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ball mill - Determination of the critical speed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHL407 - PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Ball mill - Determination of the critical speed # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **critical speed of a ball mill** is defined as the theoretical rotational speed (in revolutions per unit time) at which the centrifugal force acting on a grinding ball (or particle) at the mill wall just balances the gravitational force, causing the ball to remain pinned to the inner surface of the mill without cascading or cataracting. Beyond this speed, the grinding media is **centrifuged** along with the rotating mill shell, and no useful grinding (impact or attrition) occurs. Mathematically, the critical speed is the speed at which the **centripetal acceleration equals gravitational acceleration** for a particle on the inner wall of the mill at the topmost point of its trajectory. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCCHL407):** > The *critical speed (Nc)* of a ball mill is the rotational speed at which a grinding ball, placed at the mill wall, is on the verge of leaving the wall due to centrifugal action. It is the upper operating limit for effective size reduction. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation Imagine you are swinging a bucket of water over your head in a vertical circle (a classic physics demonstration). - If you swing the bucket **slowly**, water spills out at the top because gravity pulls it down faster than the bucket moves — this is like the **cascading** action in a ball mill. - If you swing it **faster**, the bucket presses harder on the water, and the water stays inside even when upside down because the circular motion keeps it pinned — this is the **centrifuging** effect. - The **exact transition speed** between spilling and staying inside is the **critical speed**. Below it, balls cascade and grind; above it, balls simply rotate uselessly with the shell. > [!NOTE] > **Simple Intuition:** Critical speed is the "magic speed" at which grinding stops happening because the balls stop falling and start rotating with the drum. Below this speed = useful grinding. Above this speed = wasted energy. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following constants and metrics are essential for this experiment: - **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** **9.81 m/s²** (SI) or **32.2 ft/s²** (FPS / Imperial) - **Critical speed unit:** **rpm (revolutions per minute)** or **rev/s (revolutions per second)** - **Mill radius (R):** measured in **meters (m)** or **feet (ft)** - **Mill diameter (D = 2R):** measured in the same units as R - **Operating speed (N):** typically expressed as a **percentage of critical speed (%Nc)** in industry - **Standard operating range:** **50% to 80% of Nc** for wet grinding; **65% to 80% of Nc** for dry grinding --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Forces on a Grinding Ball Consider a single grinding ball of mass **m** pressed against the inner cylindrical wall of a ball mill of internal radius **R**, rotating at angular velocity **ω** (rad/s). The two principal forces acting on the ball are: 1. **Gravitational force (W = mg)** — acts vertically downward. 2. **Normal reaction from the mill wall (N)** — acts radially inward (toward the center of the mill). For the ball to undergo **uniform circular motion** of radius R, the net radial force must equal the **centripetal force**: $$F_{centripetal} = m \omega^{2} R$$ ## 2.2 Operating Regimes in a Ball Mill Depending on the rotational speed, the grinding media exhibits three distinct motion patterns: | Regime | Speed Range | Ball Behaviour | Grinding Action | |---|---|---|---| | **Cascading** | Low (N < ~0.6 Nc) | Balls roll down the inclined surface of the charge | Attrition / rubbing (fine grinding) | | **Cataracting** | Moderate (~0.6–0.8 Nc) | Balls are projected upward, follow a parabolic path, and impact the charge | Impact + attrition (coarse grinding) | | **Centrifuging** | N ≥ Nc | Balls are pinned to the mill wall and rotate with the shell | **No grinding** — energy is wasted | > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** The critical speed marks the boundary between *useful grinding* (cascading/cataracting) and *useless rotation* (centrifuging). This is why the experiment is conducted. ## 2.3 Why and How the Critical Speed is Derived - **Why?** Because the experiment must verify the theoretical value of Nc for a given mill diameter, and confirm the speed at which centrifuging begins. - **How?** By equating the centripetal force required for circular motion to the gravitational force (at the **critical condition**, the normal reaction N → 0, meaning the wall is just about to lose contact with the ball). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The following table consolidates all essential equations, units, and conditions for the KTU 2024 board exam. | # | Formula | Description | Units | Conditions | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $N_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}}$ | Critical speed in rev/s (using radius R) | rev/s | R in m, g = 9.81 m/s² | | 2 | $N_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{2g}{D}}$ | Critical speed in rev/s (using diameter D = 2R) | rev/s | D in m, g = 9.81 m/s² | | 3 | $N_c = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{2g}{D}} = \dfrac{30}{\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{2g}{D}}$ | Critical speed in **rpm** | rpm | D in m, g = 9.81 m/s² | | 4 | $N_c = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}}$ | **Simplified** critical speed in rpm (SI) | rpm | D in **meters** | | 5 | $N_c = \dfrac{76.6}{\sqrt{D}}$ | Critical speed in rpm (FPS) | rpm | D in **feet** | | 6 | $\%N_c = \dfrac{N_{actual}}{N_c} \times 100$ | Operating speed as % of critical | dimensionless | 0–100 % | | 7 | $F_c = m \omega^2 R$ | Centripetal force on ball | N (Newton) | ω in rad/s, R in m | | 8 | $\omega = 2\pi N$ | Angular velocity from rotational speed | rad/s | N in rev/s | | 9 | $v = \pi D N$ | Peripheral velocity of mill | m/s | D in m, N in rev/s | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formula 4 is the most-used shortcut in KTU exams** (SI units). Remember: D must be in **meters** for the constant 42.3 to work correctly. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The concept of critical speed is fundamental to the design and operation of industrial comminution equipment: - **Ball mills** in cement, mineral, and pharmaceutical industries operate at carefully controlled fractions of Nc to maximize throughput and minimize energy wastage. - **Autogenous (AG) and Semi-Autogenous (SAG) mills** in mining use the same principle to size rocks efficiently. - **Vibratory mills and planetary mills** also use critical speed concepts — planetary mills operate *above* Nc to generate high-energy impacts. - The same physics governs **centrifuges, amusement park rides, washing machine drums**, and even **planetary gear systems**. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Critical Speed Formula **Setup:** Consider a ball of mass **m** located at the topmost point of the mill wall (12 o'clock position), where the mill is rotating counter-clockwise with angular velocity **ω** rad/s. The internal radius of the mill is **R**. **Step 1 — Identify the forces acting on the ball at the topmost point.** The two real forces are: - Weight of the ball: $W = mg$ (directed vertically downward, i.e., radially **inward** at the top) - Normal reaction from the mill wall on the ball: $N_{wall}$ (directed radially **inward** at the top, because the wall pushes the ball toward the center) **Step 2 — Apply Newton's second law in the radial direction.** The net inward radial force must equal the centripetal force required for the ball to move in a circle of radius R: $$N_{wall} + mg = m \omega^{2} R$$ **Step 3 — Apply the critical condition.** At the **critical speed**, the ball is *just* on the verge of leaving the mill wall. This means the wall is no longer pushing the ball inward — the contact is just about to break. Therefore: $$N_{wall} = 0 \quad \text{(critical condition)}$$ **Step 4 — Simplify the equation.** Substituting $N_{wall} = 0$ into the force balance: $$mg = m \omega_{c}^{2} R$$ The mass **m** cancels from both sides: $$g = \omega_{c}^{2} R$$ **Step 5 — Solve for the critical angular velocity $\omega_c$.** $$\omega_{c}^{2} = \dfrac{g}{R}$$ $$\omega_{c} = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}}$$ **Step 6 — Convert from angular velocity (rad/s) to rotational speed in rev/s.** The relationship between angular velocity and revolutions per second is: $$\omega = 2\pi N_{c}$$ where $N_c$ is in rev/s. Substituting: $$2\pi N_{c} = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}}$$ $$N_{c} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}} \quad \text{(rev/s)}$$ **Step 7 — Express in terms of mill diameter D = 2R.** Since $R = D/2$: $$N_{c} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{D/2}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{2g}{D}} \quad \text{(rev/s)}$$ **Step 8 — Convert to rpm (revolutions per minute) for engineering use.** Multiplying by 60: $$N_{c} = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{2g}{D}} = \dfrac{30}{\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{2g}{D}} \quad \text{(rpm)}$$ **Step 9 — Substitute numerical values for SI units (g = 9.81 m/s²).** $$N_{c} = \dfrac{30}{\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 9.81}{D}} = \dfrac{30}{\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{19.62}{D}}$$ Computing the leading constant: $$\dfrac{30}{\pi} \approx 9.5493$$ $$\sqrt{19.62} \approx 4.4294$$ Therefore: $$N_{c} = 9.5493 \times \dfrac{4.4294}{\sqrt{D}} = \dfrac{42.297}{\sqrt{D}} \approx \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}} \quad \text{(rpm, D in meters)}$$ **Step 10 — Final compact form.** $$\boxed{N_{c} = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}} \quad \text{rpm}, \quad D \text{ in meters (SI)}}$$ $$\boxed{N_{c} = \dfrac{76.6}{\sqrt{D}} \quad \text{rpm}, \quad D \text{ in feet (FPS)}}$$ ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Board Style) **Problem:** A ball mill has an internal diameter of **1.2 m**. Calculate: 1. The critical speed of the mill in **rpm**. 2. The recommended operating speed if the mill is to run at **65% of Nc**. **Given:** $D = 1.2 \text{ m}$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}$, operating fraction = 0.65 **Solution:** **Part 1 — Critical speed:** $$N_{c} = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}} = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{1.2}}$$ $$\sqrt{1.2} = 1.0954$$ $$N_{c} = \dfrac{42.3}{1.0954} = 38.61 \text{ rpm}$$ **Part 2 — Operating speed at 65 % of Nc:** $$N_{op} = 0.65 \times N_{c} = 0.65 \times 38.61$$ $$N_{op} = 25.10 \text{ rpm}$$ **Answer:** Critical speed ≈ **38.61 rpm**; Recommended operating speed ≈ **25.10 rpm**. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Influence of Mill Diameter on Critical Speed | Mill Diameter D (m) | R = D/2 (m) | $N_c$ (rpm, from $\frac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}}$) | Operating at 75 % Nc (rpm) | |---|---|---|---| | 0.5 | 0.25 | 59.82 | 44.86 | | 1.0 | 0.50 | 42.30 | 31.73 | | 1.2 | 0.60 | 38.61 | 28.96 | | 2.0 | 1.00 | 29.91 | 22.43 | | 3.0 | 1.50 | 24.42 | 18.32 | > [!NOTE] > **Trend Observed:** Critical speed **decreases** with increasing mill diameter. This is why industrial mills (large D) rotate slowly, while small laboratory mills may spin at much higher rpm. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Ball Motion Regimes in a Ball Mill The following block diagram illustrates the three operating regimes of a ball mill as a function of rotational speed, which is the conceptual core of this experiment. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["Rotating Ball Mill Drum"] --> nodeB{"Compare N with Nc"} nodeB -- "N less than 0.6 Nc" --> nodeC["CASCADING REGIME"] nodeB -- "N between 0.6 Nc and Nc" --> nodeD["CATARACTING REGIME"] nodeB -- "N greater than or equal to Nc" --> nodeE["CENTRIFUGING REGIME"] nodeC --> nodeF["Balls roll down the inclined surface of the charge"] nodeF --> nodeG["Grinding Action: Attrition and rubbing"] nodeG --> nodeG1["Produces FINE product"] nodeD --> nodeH["Balls projected upward follow parabolic path"] nodeH --> nodeI["Grinding Action: Impact plus attrition"] nodeI --> nodeI1["Produces MIXED product ideal for coarse grinding"] nodeE --> nodeJ["Balls pinned to wall and rotate with drum"] nodeJ --> nodeK["Grinding Action: NONE"] nodeK --> nodeK1["Wasted Energy - Stop the mill or reduce speed"] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Force Balance on a Grinding Ball at Critical Speed The following block diagram maps the force interaction on a single ball at the topmost point of the mill wall at the critical condition. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeBall["Ball of mass m
at 12 o'clock position
on mill wall"] --> nodeForces{"Force Balance
Radial direction"} nodeForces --> nodeW["Weight W equals m times g
Direction: Radially INWARD"] nodeForces --> nodeN["Normal Reaction N from wall
At critical speed N equals 0"] nodeW --> nodeEq["Equation: mg equals m times omega_c squared times R"] nodeN --> nodeEq nodeEq --> nodeSolve["Solve: omega_c equals sqrt of g over R"] nodeSolve --> nodeFinal["Critical Speed:
Nc equals 1 over 2pi times sqrt of 2g over D
equals 42.3 over sqrt of D rpm"] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Experimental Procedure Flow The following block diagram outlines the standard experimental procedure for determining the critical speed of a ball mill in the KTU PCCHL407 laboratory. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeP1["Step 1: Measure internal diameter D and length L of the ball mill"] --> nodeP2["Step 2: Fill the mill to about 50 percent volume with grinding balls of known size"] nodeP2 --> nodeP3["Step 3: Set the mill speed controller to a low starting value"] nodeP3 --> nodeP4["Step 4: Switch on the mill and observe the motion of the balls through the transparent window"] nodeP4 --> nodeP5{"Is centrifuging observed?"} nodeP5 -- "NO" --> nodeP6["Increase speed in small increments and repeat observation"] nodeP6 --> nodeP5 nodeP5 -- "YES" --> nodeP7["Record the speed as the experimental critical speed"] nodeP7 --> nodeP8["Step 5: Calculate the theoretical critical speed using Nc equals 42.3 over sqrt of D"] nodeP8 --> nodeP9["Step 6: Compare experimental value with theoretical value"] nodeP9 --> nodeP10["Step 7: Calculate the percentage deviation and report"] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Force Analysis Geometry The following diagram explains the geometric reasoning behind the force balance for the critical condition. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeG1["Mill cross section: circle of radius R"] --> nodeG2["Ball at top of mill, 12 o'clock position"] nodeG2 --> nodeG3["Centripetal direction points DOWNWARD toward center"] nodeG3 --> nodeG4["Gravity mg acts DOWNWARD, same direction as centripetal"] nodeG4 --> nodeG5["Normal N from wall acts INWARD on the ball"] nodeG5 --> nodeG6["At critical speed, N becomes zero"] nodeG6 --> nodeG7["Result: mg alone provides centripetal force"] nodeG7 --> nodeG8["Equation: mg equals m omega_c squared R"] nodeG8 --> nodeG9["Final formula: Nc equals 1 over 2pi times sqrt of g over R"] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** Define the term *critical speed* of a ball mill. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The critical speed of a ball mill is the theoretical rotational speed (in rpm) at which the centrifugal force acting on a grinding ball, located at the mill wall, is just equal to the gravitational force component acting on it. At this speed, the grinding media is on the verge of being centrifuged along with the rotating mill shell, and no useful grinding action (impact or attrition) takes place. Mathematically, it is given by: $$N_{c} = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}} \text{ rpm}$$ where **D** is the internal diameter of the mill in meters. Operating speeds are typically maintained at **50 – 80 % of Nc** to ensure effective grinding. **(3 marks)** --- ### **Q2.** List any three operating regimes of a ball mill and state the grinding action in each. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Regime | Speed Range | Grinding Action | |---|---|---| | 1. **Cascading** | $N < 0.6\,N_c$ | Attrition / rubbing between balls → fine product | | 2. **Cataracting** | $0.6\,N_c \le N < N_c$ | Impact + attrition → mixed product, ideal for coarse grinding | | 3. **Centrifuging** | $N \ge N_c$ | No grinding; balls rotate with the mill wall — energy is wasted | **(3 marks — 1 mark for naming each regime + 1 mark for action description bonus, distributed appropriately)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) ### **Module Internal Choice Provision (as per KTU 2024 ESE Pattern)** > As per the KTU 2024 End Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern for PCCHL407, the Part B question offers an *internal choice* — the student must attempt either the given question OR the alternative. Both options below are constructed to test the same Course Outcome with equal rigor. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks): Full Derivation + Numerical** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **(a)** Derive an expression for the critical speed of a ball mill in terms of its internal diameter **D** and acceleration due to gravity **g**. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A laboratory ball mill has an internal diameter of **0.8 m** and is filled with steel balls of diameter 25 mm to about 45 % of its volume. Calculate the theoretical critical speed of the mill in **rpm**. If the mill is to be operated at **70 % of the critical speed**, determine the operating speed in rpm. Comment on the suitability of this operating speed for size reduction. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Assumption (1 mark):** A single ball of mass *m* is considered to be located at the topmost point on the inner wall of the mill. The ball is in contact with the wall and moves in a circular path of radius *R = D/2*. **Step 2 — Free body diagram and force identification (1 mark):** The two forces acting on the ball at the topmost point are: - Weight $W = mg$ acting vertically downward (radially inward at the top). - Normal reaction $N_{wall}$ from the mill wall acting radially inward on the ball. **Step 3 — Newton's second law in the radial direction (2 marks):** $$N_{wall} + mg = m\omega^{2}R$$ **Step 4 — Critical condition (1 mark):** At the critical speed, the ball is just about to lose contact with the wall, so $N_{wall} = 0$. Therefore: $$mg = m\omega_{c}^{2}R \quad \Rightarrow \quad g = \omega_{c}^{2}R$$ **Step 5 — Solving for $\omega_c$ and converting to rpm (2 marks):** $$\omega_{c} = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}}$$ Since $\omega_c = 2\pi N_c$: $$N_{c} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{2g}{D}} \quad \text{(rev/s)}$$ Converting to rpm: $$N_{c} = \dfrac{30}{\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{2g}{D}}$$ Substituting $g = 9.81$ m/s²: $$N_{c} = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}} \quad \text{rpm, with D in meters}$$ --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Identify given data (1 mark):** $D = 0.8$ m, operating fraction = 70 % = 0.70, $g = 9.81$ m/s² **Step 2 — Calculate critical speed (2 marks):** $$N_{c} = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{0.8}} = \dfrac{42.3}{0.8944} = 47.29 \text{ rpm}$$ **Step 3 — Calculate operating speed (2 marks):** $$N_{op} = 0.70 \times 47.29 = 33.10 \text{ rpm}$$ **Step 4 — Comment on suitability (2 marks):** The operating speed of **33.10 rpm** corresponds to **70 % of the critical speed**, which lies well within the recommended industrial operating range of **50 – 80 % of Nc**. At this speed, the mill exhibits a **mixed cascading–cataracting** motion, which is ideal for achieving both impact and attrition grinding. The product obtained will be a mix of coarse and fine particles, suitable for most laboratory size-reduction applications. If the mill were run at exactly Nc (47.29 rpm), grinding would cease due to centrifuging, wasting electrical energy. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks): Conceptual + Numerical Alternative** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** Explain with the help of neat sketches the three different motion patterns of grinding balls inside a ball mill operating at different speeds. Indicate on the speed axis the typical ranges for cascading, cataracting, and centrifuging in terms of percentage of the critical speed $N_c$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A pilot-plant ball mill has an internal diameter of **1.5 m**. Determine: - (i) The critical speed in rpm. - (ii) The actual operating speed if the mill runs at **60 % of Nc**. - (iii) The peripheral velocity of the mill shell at the operating speed. - (iv) Whether the mill is suitable for fine grinding (which requires a cascading-dominated regime). **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Cascading regime (2 marks):** At low rotational speeds ($N < 0.6\,N_c$), the grinding balls do not leave the surface of the charge. They simply **roll down the inclined upper surface** of the ball charge as the mill rotates. The grinding action is dominated by **attrition** (rubbing), producing a **fine product**. The ball trajectory forms a smooth, wave-like pattern at the surface of the charge. **Step 2 — Cataracting regime (2 marks):** At moderate speeds ($0.6\,N_c \le N < N_c$), the balls are thrown clear of the charge, follow a **parabolic trajectory** through the air, and then fall back onto the charge, producing **impact breakage**. The grinding action is a combination of **impact and attrition**. This regime is preferred for **coarse grinding**. **Step 3 — Centrifuging regime (2 marks):** At the critical speed and above ($N \ge N_c$), the centrifugal force on the balls exceeds gravity. The balls become **pinned to the inner wall** of the mill and rotate with the shell, with no relative motion between them. **No grinding occurs**, and the energy input is entirely wasted as heat and sound. **Step 4 — Speed axis summary (1 mark):** | 0 % | 60 % $N_c$ | 100 % $N_c$ | |---|---|---| | Cascading | Cataracting | Centrifuging | --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Given data (1 mark):** $D = 1.5$ m, $g = 9.81$ m/s², operating fraction = 0.60 **Step 2 — (i) Critical speed (2 marks):** $$N_{c} = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}} = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{1.5}} = \dfrac{42.3}{1.2247} = 34.54 \text{ rpm}$$ **Step 3 — (ii) Operating speed (1 mark):** $$N_{op} = 0.60 \times 34.54 = 20.72 \text{ rpm}$$ **Step 4 — (iii) Peripheral velocity (2 marks):** $$v = \pi D N_{op} = \pi \times 1.5 \times 20.72 \quad \text{(Note: N must be in rev/s)}$$ Converting $N_{op}$ to rev/s: $$N_{op} = \dfrac{20.72}{60} = 0.3453 \text{ rev/s}$$ $$v = \pi \times 1.5 \times 0.3453 = 1.627 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 5 — (iv) Suitability for fine grinding (1 mark):** The operating speed of **20.72 rpm = 60 % of Nc** is on the **borderline between cascading and cataracting** regimes. It is acceptable for **moderate to coarse grinding** but is **not optimal for fine grinding**, which requires a **cascading-dominated** regime (typically below 50 % of Nc). For better fine-grinding performance, the mill should be operated at **40 – 50 % of Nc** to favour attrition over impact. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Board Exams:** > 1. **Unit Error in D** — Forgetting that D in the formula $N_c = \frac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}}$ must be in **meters (SI)**. Substituting D in cm or mm gives a wildly wrong answer. > 2. **Mixing up R and D** — The formula $N_c = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{R}}$ uses **R** (radius), while $N_c = \frac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}}$ uses **D** (diameter). Students often use D in the first formula or R in the second. > 3. **Forgetting to convert rev/s to rpm** — Final answers must be in **rpm** unless specifically asked otherwise. Multiply by 60. > 4. **Critical Condition Error** — In the derivation, students forget to set $N_{wall} = 0$ at the critical speed, leading to a wrong equation. > 5. **Confusing $N_c$ with operating speed $N$** — $N_c$ is the *theoretical* upper limit. The *actual* operating speed is a fraction (50 – 80 %) of $N_c$. > 6. **Skipping the assumption statement** — In derivation questions, the first mark is awarded for clearly stating that a single ball is considered at the top of the mill. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (Save This for Last-Minute Revision!):** - **Critical speed (Nc):** the rotational speed at which centrifuging of grinding media begins; above this, no grinding occurs. - **Formula (SI):** $N_c = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}}$ **rpm** with D in **meters**. - **Formula (FPS):** $N_c = \dfrac{76.6}{\sqrt{D}}$ **rpm** with D in **feet**. - **General form:** $N_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{2g}{D}}$ rev/s. - **Centripetal force equation:** $F_c = m\omega^2 R$; at critical speed, $mg = m\omega_c^2 R$. - **Angular velocity link:** $\omega = 2\pi N$ (with N in rev/s). - **Three regimes:** Cascading ($N < 0.6 Nc$, attrition, fine product), Cataracting ($0.6 Nc \le N < Nc$, impact + attrition, coarse product), Centrifuging ($N \ge Nc$, no grinding). - **Industrial operating range:** **50 – 80 % of Nc** (dry: 65 – 80 %; wet: 50 – 80 %). - **Critical speed trend:** As mill diameter D increases, critical speed Nc decreases. - **Peripheral velocity of mill:** $v = \pi D N$ (with N in rev/s). - **Units to remember:** $g = 9.81$ m/s² (SI), $g = 32.2$ ft/s² (FPS); 1 rev/s = 60 rpm. - **Key assumption in derivation:** A single ball is considered at the **12 o'clock position**; the mill wall just ceases to push the ball inward ($N_{wall} = 0$). - **Practical detection of Nc in the lab:** Increase mill speed gradually; the speed at which the outer layer of balls stops cascading/cataracting and begins to rotate with the shell is the experimental Nc.

topics.title: Leaf filter - Determination of specific cake resistance and compressibility factor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL407 - PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Leaf filter - Determination of specific cake resistance and compressibility factor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Leaf Filter – Formal Definition

A **Leaf Filter** (also called a *Moore filter* or *Kelly filter* in industrial terminology) is a batch-operated **pressure filtration device** in which the filter medium (cloth or woven metal mesh) is supported on a series of hollow, vertical, rectangular frames called **leaves**. These leaves are assembled inside a pressure shell. The slurry is fed under positive pressure; the liquid (filtrate) passes inward through the cloth into the hollow leaf and is withdrawn through a manifold, while the solid particles are deposited on the outer surface as a **filter cake**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (PCCHL407):** *A leaf filter is a pressure filter in which slurry is forced through filter cloth supported on hollow frames (leaves); the cake is built externally on the leaves and dislodged by a reverse pressure pulse or by physical scraping at the end of the cycle.*

---

### 1.2 Specific Cake Resistance ($\alpha$) — The Core Parameter

The **specific cake resistance** is the resistance offered per unit mass of dry cake deposited per unit area of the filter surface.

$$\alpha \;=\; \frac{\text{Resistance of cake of unit mass per unit area}}{} \quad \text{Units: } \textbf{m/kg}$$

A *high* $\alpha$ means the cake is dense, fine, and offers strong flow resistance. A *low* $\alpha$ means the cake is porous and permeable.

### 1.3 Compressibility Factor ($n$)

The **compressibility factor** is an empirical exponent (typically $0 \le n \le 1$) that quantifies how the cake resistance changes with the applied pressure differential.

$$\alpha \;=\; \alpha_{0}\,( \Delta P )^{n}$$

- $n = 0$ → Cake is **incompressible** (rigid particles, e.g., sand, coarse crystals). $\alpha$ does not change with $\Delta P$.
- $n \to 1$ → Cake is **highly compressible** (soft flocs, biological sludges). $\alpha$ rises sharply with $\Delta P$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical constants used in this experiment:**
> - Viscosity of filtrate $\mu$ — assumed equal to water at lab temperature $\approx \mathbf{1.0 \times 10^{-3}\; Pa\cdot s}$
> - Filter area $A$ — measured directly from leaf dimensions (typically $\mathbf{0.05\text{–}0.25\; m^{2}}$ for lab-scale units)
> - Slurry concentration $c$ — dry solids mass per unit volume of filtrate $\mathbf{(kg/m^{3})}$

---

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "The Tea Bag and the Press"

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine a tea bag dipped in hot water and gently squeezed.**
> 1. The tea leaves collect on the inside surface of the bag, forming a **cake**.
> 2. As you squeeze harder (higher $\Delta P$), the leaves compact and the liquid has a harder time passing through → the cake becomes **more resistive**.
> 3. If the leaves were hard pebbles (incompressible), squeezing harder wouldn't change their permeability much → $n \approx 0$.
> 4. If the leaves were squishy, gel-like material (compressible), they'd flatten and clog the bag more under pressure → $n$ approaches 1.
>
> The leaf filter works on exactly this principle — except we measure *how much* the resistance changes with pressure, and that "how much" is quantified by the **specific cake resistance $\alpha$** and the **compressibility factor $n$**.

---

### 1.5 Relevance of the Experiment in Particle Technology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this experiment matters (Engineering Utility):**
> - **Filter Sizing**: Engineers use $\alpha$ and $n$ to design industrial filters (rotary drum, plate-and-frame, leaf).
> - **Equipment Selection**: Knowing $n$ tells you whether a **vacuum filter** (low $\Delta P$) is sufficient or a **pressure filter** (high $\Delta P$) is needed.
> - **Process Optimization**: For compressible cakes ($n > 0.3$), higher pressure is counterproductive because cake resistance grows faster than the driving force → there exists an **optimum $\Delta P$**.
> - **Industrial Examples**: Sugar refining, pharmaceutical API crystallization, wastewater sludge dewatering, mineral processing tailings.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Filtration Resistance Model

Filtration flow is described by analogy with **Ohm's law for fluids** (Darcy's Law):

$$\text{Flow rate} \;=\; \frac{\text{Driving force (pressure)}}{\text{Total resistance (viscous)}}$$

The **total resistance** to flow is the sum of:
1. **Filter-medium resistance** ($R_{m}$) — resistance of the cloth itself.
2. **Cake resistance** ($R_{c}$) — resistance of the deposited solids, which grows with time as more cake accumulates.

$$\text{Total resistance} \;=\; R_{m} + R_{c}$$

The cake resistance is proportional to the **mass of dry solids deposited** per unit area ($w$):

$$R_{c} \;=\; \alpha \cdot w$$

Since $w$ grows linearly with filtrate volume $V$:

$$w \;=\; \frac{c \cdot V}{A}$$

where $c$ is the slurry concentration (mass of dry solids per volume of filtrate). Combining:

$$R_{c} \;=\; \alpha \cdot \frac{c \cdot V}{A}$$

---

### 2.2 The General Differential Filtration Equation

Applying Darcy's law with total resistance $R_{m} + \alpha c V / A$:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} \;=\; \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot \left( R_{m} + \dfrac{\alpha \, c \, V}{A} \right)} \;=\; \frac{A^{2}\, \Delta P}{\mu \left( \alpha \, c \, V + R_{m}\, A \right)}$$

This is the **starting point** for all constant-pressure and constant-rate filtration analysis.

---

### 2.3 Constant-Pressure Filtration — Integrated Form

Separating variables and integrating from $0 \to V$ and $0 \to t$ (assuming $R_{m}$ and $\alpha$ are constant at a given $\Delta P$):

$$\int_{0}^{V}\! \left( \alpha \, c \, V + R_{m}\, A \right) dV \;=\; \frac{A^{2}\, \Delta P}{\mu}\! \int_{0}^{t}\! dt$$

$$\alpha \, c \, \frac{V^{2}}{2} + R_{m}\, A\, V \;=\; \frac{A^{2}\, \Delta P}{\mu}\, t$$

Dividing throughout by $V \cdot A \cdot \Delta P / \mu$:

$$\frac{t}{V} \;=\; \underbrace{\left( \frac{\mu \, \alpha \, c}{2\, A^{2}\, \Delta P} \right)}_{\text{slope } m} \cdot V \;+\; \underbrace{\left( \frac{\mu \, R_{m}}{A\, \Delta P} \right)}_{\text{intercept } b}$$

This is the **famous linear $t/V$ vs $V$ relationship** at constant pressure.

---

### 2.4 Determination of $\alpha$ and $R_{m}$ from a Single Run

From slope and intercept of the $t/V$ vs $V$ plot:

$$m \;=\; \frac{\mu \, \alpha \, c}{2\, A^{2}\, \Delta P} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \boxed{\;\alpha \;=\; \frac{2\, m\, A^{2}\, \Delta P}{\mu \, c}\;}$$

$$b \;=\; \frac{\mu \, R_{m}}{A\, \Delta P} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \boxed{\;R_{m} \;=\; \frac{b\, A\, \Delta P}{\mu}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always express $m$ in **seconds per m⁶** and $b$ in **seconds per m³** because the plot is $t/V$ (s/m³) versus $V$ (m³). Unit conversion errors are the #1 cause of losing marks in filtration calculations.

---

### 2.5 Compressibility — Multiple-Pressure Runs

The cake resistance depends on $\Delta P$ because cake particles may deform and rearrange:

$$\alpha \;=\; \alpha_{0}\, (\Delta P)^{n}$$

Taking logarithms:

$$\ln \alpha \;=\; \ln \alpha_{0} + n \, \ln (\Delta P)$$

A plot of $\ln \alpha$ vs $\ln \Delta P$ gives:
- **Slope** = $n$ (compressibility factor)
- **Intercept** = $\ln \alpha_{0}$ → $\alpha_{0} = e^{\text{intercept}}$ (specific cake resistance at $\Delta P = 1$ unit)

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units / Typical Range |
|---|---------|---------------|------------------------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{A^{2}\, \Delta P}{\mu (\alpha c V + R_{m} A)}$ | General differential filtration equation | $\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$ |
| 2 | $\dfrac{t}{V} = \dfrac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^{2} \Delta P}\, V + \dfrac{\mu R_{m}}{A \Delta P}$ | Constant-pressure linear form; slope $m$, intercept $b$ | $t/V$ in $\text{s/m}^{3}$, $V$ in $\text{m}^{3}$ |
| 3 | $\alpha = \dfrac{2 m A^{2} \Delta P}{\mu c}$ | Specific cake resistance from one run | $\text{m/kg}$ |
| 4 | $R_{m} = \dfrac{b A \Delta P}{\mu}$ | Filter-medium resistance from one run | $\text{m}^{-1}$ |
| 5 | $\alpha = \alpha_{0} (\Delta P)^{n}$ | Empirical compressibility relation | $\alpha_{0}$ in $\text{m/kg}$, $\Delta P$ in $\text{Pa}$, $n$ dimensionless |
| 6 | $\ln \alpha = \ln \alpha_{0} + n \ln \Delta P$ | Linearized form for plotting $n$ and $\alpha_{0}$ | dimensionless plot |
| 7 | $w = \dfrac{cV}{A}$ | Mass of dry cake per unit area at filtrate volume $V$ | $\text{kg/m}^{2}$ |
| 8 | $\text{Optimum } \Delta P \;\propto\; \left( \dfrac{1-n}{n} \right)$ | Qualitative; for $n > 0.3$, very high $\Delta P$ is harmful | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 9 | $\mu_{\text{water}} \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\, e^{-0.023(T-20)}$ at $T\,(^{\circ}\text{C})$ | Viscosity of water (if filtrate = water) | $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ |
| 10 | $V_{\text{eq}} = \dfrac{A^{2}\, \Delta P}{\mu \alpha c}\, t$ | Implicit transcendental equation (full non-linear form) | $\text{m}^{3}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** For an **incompressible cake** ($n=0$), doubling $\Delta P$ doubles the throughput — exactly what we want. For a **compressible cake** ($n=0.6$), doubling $\Delta P$ increases throughput by only $\Delta P^{1-n} = 2^{0.4} \approx 1.32\times$ — much less effective. This is why vacuum filters are often preferred for compressible biological sludges.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Constant-Pressure Filtration Equation

**Starting point (Darcy's law applied to filtration):**

$$\frac{dV}{dt} \;=\; \frac{A^{2}\, \Delta P}{\mu \left( \alpha \, c \, V + R_{m}\, A \right)} \qquad \text{(Eq. A)}$$

**Step 1 — Rearrange to separate variables.** Multiply both sides by the denominator and bring $dV$ to the left:

$$\left( \alpha \, c \, V + R_{m}\, A \right) dV \;=\; \frac{A^{2}\, \Delta P}{\mu}\, dt$$

**Step 2 — Integrate both sides.** Lower limits: $V=0$ at $t=0$. Upper limits: $V=V$ at $t=t$.

$$\int_{0}^{V} \left( \alpha \, c \, V + R_{m}\, A \right) dV \;=\; \frac{A^{2}\, \Delta P}{\mu}\! \int_{0}^{t}\! dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integrals.**

$$\alpha \, c \, \frac{V^{2}}{2} \;+\; R_{m}\, A\, V \;=\; \frac{A^{2}\, \Delta P}{\mu}\, t$$

*Text: This is the integrated form giving a quadratic relation between $t$ and $V$.*

**Step 4 — Divide both sides by $V$ to obtain a linear $t/V$ form.**

$$\frac{t}{V} \;=\; \frac{\mu \, \alpha \, c}{2\, A^{2}\, \Delta P}\, V \;+\; \frac{\mu \, R_{m}}{A\, \Delta P}$$

*Text: Now $t/V$ is linear in $V$. The slope $m$ and intercept $b$ are functions of the operating pressure $\Delta P$, slurry properties, and filter geometry.*

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Expressions for $\alpha$ and $R_{m}$

From the linear $t/V$ vs $V$ plot:

$$\text{slope } m \;=\; \frac{\mu \, \alpha \, c}{2\, A^{2}\, \Delta P} \qquad \text{(Eq. B)}$$

$$\text{intercept } b \;=\; \frac{\mu \, R_{m}}{A\, \Delta P} \qquad \text{(Eq. C)}$$

**Step 1 — Solve Eq. B for $\alpha$.** Multiply both sides by $2 A^{2} \Delta P / (\mu c)$:

$$\alpha \;=\; \frac{2\, m\, A^{2}\, \Delta P}{\mu \, c}$$

**Step 2 — Solve Eq. C for $R_{m}$.** Multiply both sides by $A \Delta P / \mu$:

$$R_{m} \;=\; \frac{b\, A\, \Delta P}{\mu}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Compressibility Factor from $\alpha$ vs $\Delta P$

**Empirical starting equation:**

$$\alpha \;=\; \alpha_{0}\, (\Delta P)^{n}$$

**Step 1 — Take natural logarithm of both sides.** Use $\ln(xy) = \ln x + \ln y$ and $\ln(x^{a}) = a \ln x$:

$$\ln \alpha \;=\; \ln \alpha_{0} + n \, \ln (\Delta P)$$

**Step 2 — Compare with $Y = mX + C$ form.** Here $Y = \ln \alpha$, $X = \ln \Delta P$:

$$Y \;=\; n \cdot X + \ln \alpha_{0}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation of slope and intercept.**
- Slope of $\ln \alpha$ vs $\ln \Delta P$ plot = $n$ (compressibility factor, dimensionless).
- Intercept = $\ln \alpha_{0}$ → so $\alpha_{0} = e^{\text{intercept}}$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (Numerical Demonstration)

**Given Data for a leaf filter experiment** ($A = 0.10\,\text{m}^{2}$, slurry conc. $c = 30\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$, $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa\cdot s}$):

| Run | $\Delta P$ (kPa) | Slope $m$ ($\text{s/m}^{6}$) | Intercept $b$ ($\text{s/m}^{3}$) |
|-----|-------------------|-------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| 1   | 50                | $5.20 \times 10^{6}$          | $2.80 \times 10^{4}$             |
| 2   | 100               | $7.50 \times 10^{6}$          | $2.90 \times 10^{4}$             |
| 3   | 200               | $10.6 \times 10^{6}$          | $3.00 \times 10^{4}$             |
| 4   | 400               | $15.0 \times 10^{6}$          | $3.10 \times 10^{4}$             |

**Step 1 — Calculate $\alpha$ for each run** using $\alpha = 2 m A^{2} \Delta P / (\mu c)$.

For Run 1 ($\Delta P = 50{,}000$ Pa):

$$\alpha_{1} \;=\; \frac{2 \times (5.20 \times 10^{6}) \times (0.10)^{2} \times 50{,}000}{(1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times 30}$$

$$\alpha_{1} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 5.20 \times 10^{6} \times 0.01 \times 50{,}000}{0.03} \;=\; \frac{5.20 \times 10^{9}}{0.03} \;\approx\; 1.73 \times 10^{11}\,\text{m/kg}$$

For Run 2 ($\Delta P = 100{,}000$ Pa):

$$\alpha_{2} \;=\; \frac{2 \times (7.50 \times 10^{6}) \times 0.01 \times 100{,}000}{0.03} \;=\; \frac{1.50 \times 10^{10}}{0.03} \;\approx\; 5.00 \times 10^{11}\,\text{m/kg}$$

For Run 3 ($\Delta P = 200{,}000$ Pa):

$$\alpha_{3} \;=\; \frac{2 \times (10.6 \times 10^{6}) \times 0.01 \times 200{,}000}{0.03} \;\approx\; 1.41 \times 10^{12}\,\text{m/kg}$$

For Run 4 ($\Delta P = 400{,}000$ Pa):

$$\alpha_{4} \;=\; \frac{2 \times (15.0 \times 10^{6}) \times 0.01 \times 400{,}000}{0.03} \;\approx\; 4.00 \times 10^{12}\,\text{m/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Construct log–log table.**

| Run | $\Delta P$ (Pa) | $\alpha$ (m/kg) | $\ln \Delta P$ | $\ln \alpha$ |
|-----|------------------|------------------|----------------|---------------|
| 1   | $5.0 \times 10^{4}$ | $1.73 \times 10^{11}$ | 10.82 | 25.57 |
| 2   | $1.0 \times 10^{5}$ | $5.00 \times 10^{11}$ | 11.51 | 26.64 |
| 3   | $2.0 \times 10^{5}$ | $1.41 \times 10^{12}$ | 12.21 | 27.97 |
| 4   | $4.0 \times 10^{5}$ | $4.00 \times 10^{12}$ | 12.90 | 29.00 |

**Step 3 — Slope of $\ln \alpha$ vs $\ln \Delta P$** (using two-point form):

$$n \;=\; \frac{\ln \alpha_{4} - \ln \alpha_{1}}{\ln \Delta P_{4} - \ln \Delta P_{1}} \;=\; \frac{29.00 - 25.57}{12.90 - 10.82} \;=\; \frac{3.43}{2.08} \;\approx\; 0.65$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\alpha_{0}$.**

$$\ln \alpha_{0} \;=\; \ln \alpha - n \ln \Delta P \;=\; 25.57 - 0.65 \times 10.82 \;=\; 18.54$$

$$\alpha_{0} \;=\; e^{18.54} \;\approx\; 1.12 \times 10^{8}\,\text{m/kg (when }\Delta P\text{ in Pa)}$$

**Conclusion:** $n \approx 0.65$ indicates a **moderately compressible cake** (typical of fine chemical or biological slurries).

---

### 3.5 Boundary-Condition and Limiting-Case Checklist

| Limit | Mathematical form | Physical meaning |
|-------|-------------------|------------------|
| $n \to 0$ | $\alpha = \alpha_{0}$ = constant | Incompressible cake (rigid particles) |
| $n \to 1$ | $\alpha \propto \Delta P$ | Highly compressible (gel-like) cake |
| $V \to 0$ | $t/V \to b$ | At start, only medium resistance dominates |
| $V \to \infty$ | $t/V \approx m V$ | Cake resistance dominates; medium negligible |
| $R_{m} \to 0$ | $t/V = m V$ only | Filter medium neglected; pure cake filtration |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Experimental Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Slurry Preparation"] --> B["Measure Concentration c"]
    B --> C["Measure Filter Area A"]
    C --> D["Assemble Leaf Filter"]
    D --> E["Set Pressure P1"]
    E --> F["Record V vs t"]
    F --> G["Plot t per V vs V"]
    G --> H["Determine Slope m1 and Intercept b1"]
    H --> I["Compute alpha1 and Rm1"]
    I --> J["Change to Pressure P2"]
    J --> K["Repeat for P3 and P4"]
    K --> L["Tabulate alpha vs DeltaP"]
    L --> M["Plot ln alpha vs ln DeltaP"]
    M --> N["Slope Equals n"]
    M --> O["Intercept Equals ln alpha0"]
    N --> P["Compressibility Factor n"]
    O --> Q["Reference Resistance alpha0"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Leaf Filter Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN["Input Side"]
        S1["Slurry Tank"]
        P1["Feed Pump"]
    end

    subgraph FILT["Pressure Shell Vessel"]
        L1["Leaf 1 with Cloth"]
        L2["Leaf 2 with Cloth"]
        L3["Leaf 3 with Cloth"]
        L4["Leaf 4 with Cloth"]
        MAN["Filtrate Manifold"]
    end

    subgraph OUT["Output Side"]
        F1["Filtrate Collection"]
        DR["Cake Discharge"]
        G1["Pressure Gauge"]
    end

    S1 --> P1
    P1 --> L1
    P1 --> L2
    P1 --> L3
    P1 --> L4
    L1 --> MAN
    L2 --> MAN
    L3 --> MAN
    L4 --> MAN
    MAN --> F1
    L1 -.Builds Cake.-> L1
    L2 -.Builds Cake.-> L2
    L3 -.Builds Cake.-> L3
    L4 -.Builds Cake.-> L4
    L1 --> DR
    G1 --> FILT
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Calculation Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1["Constant Pressure Run at DeltaP1"] --> S1["Plot t per V vs V"]
    S1 --> M1["Slope m1 Intercept b1"]
    M1 --> CA1["alpha1 equals 2 m1 A squared DeltaP1 over mu c"]
    M1 --> CR1["Rm1 equals b1 A DeltaP1 over mu"]

    R2["Constant Pressure Run at DeltaP2"] --> S2["Plot t per V vs V"]
    S2 --> M2["Slope m2 Intercept b2"]
    M2 --> CA2["alpha2"]

    R3["Constant Pressure Run at DeltaP3"] --> S3["Plot t per V vs V"]
    S3 --> CA3["alpha3"]

    R4["Constant Pressure Run at DeltaP4"] --> S4["Plot t per V vs V"]
    S4 --> CA4["alpha4"]

    CA1 --> LL["Combine alpha values"]
    CA2 --> LL
    CA3 --> LL
    CA4 --> LL

    LL --> PL["Plot ln alpha vs ln DeltaP"]
    PL --> SL["Slope gives n Compressibility Factor"]
    PL --> IC["Intercept gives ln alpha0"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each rectangular block represents a physical operation or a mathematical transformation. The arrows show the **direction of data flow** — for example, the raw $V$–$t$ data feeds into the slope–intercept calculation, which then feeds into the $\alpha$ calculation. The final "compression block" combines all $\alpha$ values across multiple pressures to extract $n$ and $\alpha_{0}$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
