**Define specific cake resistance. What are its SI units, and what does a high value of $\alpha$ signify?**

**Model Answer:**

The **specific cake resistance** ($\alpha$) is defined as the resistance offered by the filter cake per unit mass of dry solids deposited per unit area of the filter medium.

$$\boxed{\alpha \;=\; \frac{\text{Cake resistance per unit area}}{\text{Mass of dry cake per unit area}}} \quad [\text{SI Units: } \textbf{m/kg}]$$

A **high $\alpha$** indicates that the cake is dense and offers high resistance to filtrate flow — typically caused by fine particles, compressible flocs, or a tightly packed structure. Such cakes require higher pressure differentials for filtration or demand precoat/cake-release aids.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the significance of the compressibility factor $n$. What do the values $n = 0$ and $n \to 1$ represent?**

**Model Answer:**

The compressibility factor **$n$** is an empirical exponent in the relation $\alpha = \alpha_{0}(\Delta P)^{n}$ that quantifies how the cake's resistance changes with applied pressure.

- **$n = 0$:** Cake is **incompressible** — particles are rigid (e.g., sand, sugar crystals, calcium carbonate). $\alpha$ does **not** vary with $\Delta P$. Higher pressure always proportionally increases throughput.
- **$n \to 1$:** Cake is **highly compressible** — soft flocs, biological sludges, gelatinous precipitates. $\alpha$ grows nearly linearly with $\Delta P$, so the gain in throughput is largely nullified by the compaction of the cake.
- **$0 < n < 1$:** **Partially compressible** cake (most real systems). The experimental task is to determine the actual value of $n$ from a $\ln\alpha$ vs $\ln\Delta P$ plot.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

## **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]`

**(a)** Derive the constant-pressure filtration equation starting from Darcy's law. Clearly define each term and show how the slope and intercept of the $t/V$ vs $V$ plot relate to specific cake resistance $\alpha$ and medium resistance $R_{m}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Starting equation (general filtration equation):**

$$\frac{dV}{dt} \;=\; \frac{A^{2}\, \Delta P}{\mu \left( \alpha \, c \, V + R_{m}\, A \right)}$$

where $V$ = filtrate volume (m³), $t$ = time (s), $A$ = filter area (m²), $\Delta P$ = pressure drop (Pa), $\mu$ = filtrate viscosity (Pa·s), $c$ = slurry concentration (kg/m³), $\alpha$ = specific cake resistance (m/kg), $R_{m}$ = medium resistance (m⁻¹).

**Step 2 — Rearrange:**

$$\left( \alpha \, c \, V + R_{m}\, A \right) dV \;=\; \frac{A^{2}\, \Delta P}{\mu}\, dt$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $V=0$ at $t=0$:**

$$\frac{\alpha c V^{2}}{2} + R_{m} A V \;=\; \frac{A^{2}\, \Delta P}{\mu}\, t$$

**Step 4 — Divide by $V$:**

$$\frac{t}{V} \;=\; \underbrace{\left( \frac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^{2}\, \Delta P} \right)}_{\text{slope } m} V \;+\; \underbrace{\left( \frac{\mu R_{m}}{A\, \Delta P} \right)}_{\text{intercept } b}$$

**Step 5 — Inversion for $\alpha$ and $R_{m}$:**

$$\boxed{\;\alpha \;=\; \frac{2\, m\, A^{2}\, \Delta P}{\mu \, c}\;} \qquad \boxed{\;R_{m} \;=\; \frac{b\, A\, \Delta P}{\mu}\;}$$

The slope $m$ and intercept $b$ are obtained graphically from $t/V$ vs $V$, and these formulas give $\alpha$ and $R_{m}$ once $A$, $c$, $\mu$, and $\Delta P$ are known.

---

**(b)** A leaf-filter experiment with filter area $A = 0.15\,\text{m}^{2}$, slurry concentration $c = 25\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$, and filtrate viscosity $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa\cdot s}$ gives a slope $m = 4.80 \times 10^{6}\,\text{s/m}^{6}$ at $\Delta P = 150\,\text{kPa}$. A second run at $\Delta P = 300\,\text{kPa}$ gives $m = 6.10 \times 10^{6}\,\text{s/m}^{6}$. Compute the specific cake resistance for both runs and the compressibility factor $n$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Run 1:** $\Delta P_{1} = 150{,}000$ Pa, $m_{1} = 4.80 \times 10^{6}\,\text{s/m}^{6}$

$$\alpha_{1} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 4.80 \times 10^{6} \times (0.15)^{2} \times 150{,}000}{1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 25}$$

$$\alpha_{1} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 4.80 \times 10^{6} \times 0.0225 \times 150{,}000}{0.025} \;=\; \frac{3.24 \times 10^{10}}{0.025} \;\approx\; 1.30 \times 10^{12}\,\text{m/kg}$$

**Run 2:** $\Delta P_{2} = 300{,}000$ Pa, $m_{2} = 6.10 \times 10^{6}\,\text{s/m}^{6}$

$$\alpha_{2} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 6.10 \times 10^{6} \times 0.0225 \times 300{,}000}{0.025} \;\approx\; 3.29 \times 10^{12}\,\text{m/kg}$$

**Compressibility factor** from $\alpha = \alpha_{0} (\Delta P)^{n}$:

$$n \;=\; \frac{\ln(\alpha_{2}/\alpha_{1})}{\ln(\Delta P_{2}/\Delta P_{1})} \;=\; \frac{\ln(3.29/1.30)}{\ln(300{,}000/150{,}000)} \;=\; \frac{\ln(2.53)}{\ln(2)} \;=\; \frac{0.929}{0.693} \;\approx\; 1.34$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall – Part B(b):**
> The value $n > 1$ here is unusual (physically it should lie between 0 and 1). In a real exam, this signals either a calculation slip, missing unit conversion (Pa vs kPa), or wrong slope value. Always sanity-check that $0 \le n \le 1$. A correct exam problem would have $n \approx 0.3\text{–}0.7$. Students who blindly report $n > 1$ without comment will lose 1 mark.

---

## **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]`

**(a)** Describe with a neat block diagram the principle and construction of a leaf filter. List the experimental steps to determine specific cake resistance and compressibility factor. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** A leaf filter operates on the principle of **pressure filtration**, where slurry is pumped under positive pressure into a closed vessel containing hollow frames (leaves) covered with filter cloth. Filtrate passes inward through the cloth into the leaf interior and exits via a manifold; solids build up as a cake on the outside of each leaf.

**Construction (Block List):**

1. **Pressure shell** — cylindrical vessel that withstands the operating pressure.
2. **Leaves** — vertical hollow frames (rectangular or circular), each supporting the filter cloth on both sides.
3. **Filter medium** — woven cloth (polypropylene, cotton) or sintered metal mesh.
4. **Filtrate manifold** — common outlet collecting filtrate from all leaves.
5. **Slurry inlet** — top entry for feed.
6. **Cake discharge port** — bottom for cake removal.
7. **Pressure gauge and safety valve** — for monitoring and protection.

**Experimental Procedure:**

1. Prepare slurry of known concentration $c$ (measure by drying a known volume).
2. Measure filter area $A$ from leaf dimensions.
3. Assemble filter, wet the cloth, and close the shell.
4. Set the desired pressure $\Delta P_{1}$ on the pressure regulator.
5. Open the slurry inlet; start the stopwatch.
6. Record filtrate volume $V$ at fixed time intervals $t$.
7. Repeat for at least 3–4 different pressures.
8. Plot $t/V$ vs $V$ for each run → determine $m$ and $b$.
9. Compute $\alpha$ for each run using $\alpha = 2 m A^{2} \Delta P / (\mu c)$.
10. Plot $\ln \alpha$ vs $\ln \Delta P$ → slope = $n$, intercept = $\ln \alpha_{0}$.

---

**(b)** In a leaf-filter experiment, the following $t/V$ vs $V$ slopes were obtained:

| $\Delta P$ (kPa) | $m$ ($\text{s/m}^{6}$) |
|-------------------|-------------------------|
| 100               | $3.20 \times 10^{6}$    |
| 200               | $4.55 \times 10^{6}$    |
| 400               | $6.45 \times 10^{6}$    |

Given $A = 0.20\,\text{m}^{2}$, $c = 40\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$, $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa\cdot s}$. Calculate $\alpha$ for each run, find the compressibility factor $n$ and the reference specific cake resistance $\alpha_{0}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Run 1** ($\Delta P_{1} = 10^{5}$ Pa):

$$\alpha_{1} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 3.20 \times 10^{6} \times (0.20)^{2} \times 10^{5}}{10^{-3} \times 40} \;=\; \frac{2.56 \times 10^{10}}{0.04} \;=\; 6.40 \times 10^{11}\,\text{m/kg}$$

**Run 2** ($\Delta P_{2} = 2 \times 10^{5}$ Pa):

$$\alpha_{2} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 4.55 \times 10^{6} \times 0.04 \times 2 \times 10^{5}}{0.04} \;=\; 1.82 \times 10^{12}\,\text{m/kg}$$

**Run 3** ($\Delta P_{3} = 4 \times 10^{5}$ Pa):

$$\alpha_{3} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 6.45 \times 10^{6} \times 0.04 \times 4 \times 10^{5}}{0.04} \;=\; 5.16 \times 10^{12}\,\text{m/kg}$$

**Log table:**

| Run | $\Delta P$ (Pa) | $\alpha$ (m/kg) | $\ln \Delta P$ | $\ln \alpha$ |
|-----|------------------|------------------|----------------|---------------|
| 1   | $1.0 \times 10^{5}$ | $6.40 \times 10^{11}$ | 11.51 | 27.19 |
| 2   | $2.0 \times 10^{5}$ | $1.82 \times 10^{12}$ | 12.21 | 28.23 |
| 3   | $4.0 \times 10^{5}$ | $5.16 \times 10^{12}$ | 12.90 | 29.27 |

**Compressibility factor** (using Run 1 and Run 3 to minimize error):

$$n \;=\; \frac{29.27 - 27.19}{12.90 - 11.51} \;=\; \frac{2.08}{1.39} \;\approx\; 1.50$$

**Reference cake resistance** (using Run 1):

$$\ln \alpha_{0} \;=\; \ln \alpha_{1} - n \ln \Delta P_{1} \;=\; 27.19 - 1.50 \times 11.51 \;=\; 9.93$$

$$\alpha_{0} \;=\; e^{9.93} \;\approx\; 2.04 \times 10^{4}\,\text{m/kg}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch trap** (–2 marks): Using $\Delta P$ in kPa instead of Pa in the $\alpha$ formula gives wrong answers by a factor of 1000. Always convert to **SI base units (Pa)** before substitution.
> 2. **Forgetting to square $A$** (–2 marks): The formula has $A^{2}$, not $A$. This is one of the most common errors.
> 3. **Mixing up slope and intercept** (–1 mark): Slope gives $\alpha$, intercept gives $R_{m}$.
> 4. **Not commenting on cake type** (–1 mark): Always state whether the cake is compressible ($n > 0.3$) or incompressible ($n \le 0.3$) as part of the conclusion.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Leaf Filter Lab (Module Experiment 2)**
>
> - **Apparatus:** Leaf filter (vertical leaves in pressure shell), slurry tank, pump, pressure gauge, measuring cylinder, stopwatch.
> - **Key measurements:** Filtrate volume $V$ at time $t$; multiple runs at different $\Delta P$ (typically 3–5).
> - **Plot #1:** $t/V$ (s/m³) **vs** $V$ (m³) → linear; slope $m$, intercept $b$.
> - **Plot #2:** $\ln \alpha$ (dimensionless) **vs** $\ln \Delta P$ (dimensionless, with $\Delta P$ in Pa) → slope $n$, intercept $\ln \alpha_{0}$.
> - **Core formulas (memorize these 3):**
>   1. $\dfrac{t}{V} = \dfrac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^{2} \Delta P}\, V + \dfrac{\mu R_{m}}{A \Delta P}$
>   2. $\alpha = \dfrac{2 m A^{2} \Delta P}{\mu c}$
>   3. $\ln \alpha = \ln \alpha_{0} + n \ln \Delta P$
> - **Sanity checks:**
>   - $\alpha$ in the range $\mathbf{10^{10} \text{–} 10^{13}\,m/kg}$ for most slurries.
>   - $n$ must satisfy $\mathbf{0 \le n \le 1}$.
>   - $\alpha$ should **increase** with $\Delta P$ (except for $n=0$ incompressible case).
> - **Lab viva favorites:**
>   - "Why is $t/V$ plotted and not $V/t$?" — *Because $V/t$ is the instantaneous flow rate, which decreases with time at constant $\Delta P$; $t/V$ linearizes the data.*
>   - "What does $\alpha_{0}$ represent?" — *Specific cake resistance at unit pressure differential $\Delta P = 1$ Pa.*
>   - "Why does cake resistance grow during filtration?" — *Because more solids accumulate on the medium, increasing the cake thickness and hence $R_{c} = \alpha w$.*
> - **Common lab errors:** Air-locked manifold (gives erratic slopes), cloth not properly seated (leakage), pressure not stabilized before timing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

Studies on Mineral jig, Wilfley table and Continuous thickener
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL407 - PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Studies on Mineral jig, Wilfley table and Continuous thickener

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Mineral Jig

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A **Mineral Jig** is a mechanical gravity concentration device that separates particles of different densities (typically used to concentrate heavy minerals like galena, cassiterite, hematite, etc. from lighter gangue minerals) by introducing a pulsating vertical column of water through a bed of particles resting on a perforated screen (jig bed).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine washing a mixed bowl of rice and small stones in a bucket of water with a rhythmic up-and-down motion. With each pulse, the heavy stones quickly sink to the bottom, while the lighter rice grains float near the top. By the time the water settles, you can scoop off the rice from above and retrieve the stones from the bottom. **A mineral jig does precisely this — at industrial scale, thousands of times per minute** — separating ore particles purely based on their specific gravity differences.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Operating Standard:**
> Jigging operates on the principle of **hindered settling**, where the terminal settling velocity of a particle in a fluidized bed is governed by its density relative to the surrounding suspension. Typical jig frequency: **60–300 pulsations per minute**; amplitude: **2–25 mm**.

---

## 1.2 Wilfley Table

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> The **Wilfley Shaking Table** is a concentration device consisting of a slightly inclined, riffled (grooved) deck that undergoes a differential asymmetric shaking motion along its longitudinal axis. It separates fine particles (typically **75 µm to 6 mm**) by exploiting the combined effects of gravity, flowing water (launder), and the table's reciprocating motion.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Picture a tilted wooden tray covered with thin parallel wooden slats, with a gentle stream of water flowing down it. If you tap one end of the tray rhythmically while the water flows, a ball bearing placed on the tray will *crawl forward* along the tapping direction and roll *across* the slats due to the water. A small cork, however, will mostly drift with the water sideways and lag behind longitudinally. **A Wilfley table behaves identically** — heavy, dense particles move along the riffles (longitudinal), while light particles are washed diagonally by the water film (lateral).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Perpendicular Forces at Play:**
> - **Longitudinal motion (ΔL):** caused by the asymmetric forward–backward shake (differential acceleration).
> - **Lateral motion (ΔB):** caused by the water film flowing over the inclined riffled deck.
> Particles separate into a **concentrate band (heavy)**, **middling band**, and **tailings band (light)**.

---

## 1.3 Continuous Thickener

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A **Continuous Thickener** is a large, circular, slowly-rotating sedimentation tank (often with a central feed well, rotating rake arms, and an overflow launder at the periphery) used to separate a dilute feed slurry into a clarified overflow liquor and a thickened underflow slurry of higher solids concentration, operating under steady-state conditions via gravity settling.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a wide, shallow swimming pool filled with muddy water. If you let it sit undisturbed, the mud slowly sinks to the bottom, leaving clear water above. In a continuous thickener, the process never pauses — muddy water is continuously fed at the **center**, clear water continuously overflows at the **edge**, and concentrated mud is continuously scraped out from the **bottom**. Slow rotating rakes gently guide the settled sludge toward a central discharge cone without disturbing the sedimentation zones.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Functional Zones Inside a Thickener:**
> 1. **Clarification Zone (top):** free-settling of the last fine particles.
> 2. **Sedimentation/Compression Zone (middle):** hindered settling; particles approach critical concentration.
> 3. **Compression Zone (bottom):** slow consolidation under its own weight; rakes aid compression.

---

## 1.4 Comparative Snapshot

| Equipment | Driving Force | Feed Size | Basis of Separation | Typical Recovery |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mineral Jig | Pulsating water + gravity | 0.5 mm – 25 mm | Density | 70–90 % |
| Wilfley Table | Shaking + water film + gravity | 75 µm – 6 mm | Density + Size + Shape | 80–95 % |
| Continuous Thickener | Gravity settling | 1 µm – 100 µm | Density (clarification/concentration) | 95–99 % (solids) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mineral Jig — Operational Logic

**Step-by-step stratification mechanism:**

1. **Pulsation Up-stroke (Dilatation):** Water flows upward through the bed, lifting and fluidizing all particles. The bed dilates; particles momentarily lose contact.
2. **Pulsation Down-stroke (Consolidation):** Water is displaced downward; the bed consolidates. **Lighter particles** are slow to fall through the openings and are displaced upward.
3. **Suction (Hindered Settling) Phase:** During suction, water rushes downward, dragging small and light particles with it — but **denser particles** overcome the suction and sink to form a bottom layer.
4. **Repetition:** Over many cycles, the bed becomes stratified: **heavies at the bottom, lights at the top**.
5. **Discharge:** Bottom heavy particles (concentrate) are extracted through the screen; top light particles (tailings) overflow at the side.

**Why the bed stratifies (the core 'Why'):**
A particle in a fluidized bed has an effective settling velocity given by hindered settling equations. The greater the density difference between particle and fluidized medium, the faster it sinks. Over many cycles, this differential settling produces a sharp density gradient.

---

## 2.2 Wilfley Table — Differential Motion Theory

**Particle Trajectory on the Deck:**

A particle on the table experiences two simultaneous motions:

- **Forward (longitudinal) push:** The asymmetric crank mechanism gives the deck a *slow forward* and *quick return* stroke. Heavy particles, due to inertia, advance more during the slow forward stroke than they retreat during the rapid return → **Net forward progress**.
- **Lateral (cross-flow) wash:** Water flowing down the inclined deck carries particles sideways. Lighter particles have a lower inertia, so the water drag dominates → **Lighter particles move more sideways**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Net result:** Heavy particles (Concentrate) collect near the **discharge end** of the riffles; Light particles (Tailings) exit via the **side launder (wash water side)**. Middlings occupy the diagonal region between them.

**Deck Slope and Riffle Geometry:**
- Deck slope: **0.25° – 0.75°** (adjustable).
- Riffles: triangular, tapered, decreasing in height from feed end to concentrate end.
- Stroke length: **10–25 mm**; Stroke frequency: **240–300 strokes/min**.

---

## 2.3 Continuous Thickener — Sedimentation Theory

**Step-by-step settling logic inside the thickener:**

1. **Feed enters** the central feed well at low velocity to minimize turbulence.
2. **Solids begin to settle** by gravity; clarified liquor rises upward.
3. **Hindered settling regime:** As solids concentration increases downward, particles interfere with each other → settling velocity decreases (Kynch, Talmage-Fitch theory).
4. **Compression regime:** Below the critical concentration, particles form a continuous network; further densification occurs via slow consolidation, aided by the rotating rake arms.
5. **Overflow** exits via peripheral launder; **underflow** is pumped out from the central cone.

**The Core 'How':**
A thickener is sized based on the **limiting solids flux** (mass of solids that can pass per unit area per unit time). The diameter is determined by the **Talmage–Fitch** graphical method, which uses Kynch's sedimentation theory to find the *critical settling flux*.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **KTU 2024 Scheme exam-grade**, mathematically verified, and form the backbone of viva + numerical questions.

### A. Mineral Jig

| Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $\displaystyle R = \frac{m_c \cdot \beta}{m_f \cdot \alpha}$ | Metallurgical Recovery | % |
| $\displaystyle \text{Separation Efficiency} = R_T - R_G$ | Tromp efficiency | % |
| $\displaystyle v_t = \frac{g \, d^2 (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{18 \, \mu}$ | Stokes settling velocity (hindered) | m/s |
| $\displaystyle E_c = \frac{C_c - C_f}{C_f - C_t}$ | Concentration criterion (Gaudin) | — |

Where $m_c$ = mass of concentrate, $\beta$ = grade of concentrate, $m_f$ = mass of feed, $\alpha$ = grade of feed, $C_c, C_f, C_t$ = assay of valuable metal in conc., feed, tailing.

### B. Wilfley Table

| Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $\displaystyle \text{Recovery} = \frac{m_c \, \beta}{m_f \, \alpha} \times 100$ | Recovery | % |
| $\displaystyle \text{Grade} = \frac{\text{Mass of valuable mineral}}{\text{Total mass of concentrate}} \times 100$ | Concentrate grade | % |
| $\displaystyle \eta = \frac{\beta(\alpha - t)}{\alpha(\beta - t)} \times 100$ | Gaudin's separation efficiency | % |
| $\displaystyle Q = L \cdot W \cdot \rho_b \cdot U$ | Throughput capacity (per deck area) | kg/h |

Where $L, W$ = deck length and width, $\rho_b$ = bulk density, $U$ = feed velocity along deck, $t$ = tailing grade.

### C. Continuous Thickener

| Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $\displaystyle v = \frac{g \, d^2 (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{18 \, \mu}$ | Stokes' law (viscous regime, $Re_p \le 0.1$) | m/s |
| $\displaystyle v = 1.74 \sqrt{\frac{g \, d \, (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{\rho_f}}$ | Newton's law (turbulent regime, $Re_p \ge 1000$) | m/s |
| $\displaystyle Re_p = \frac{d \, v \, \rho_f}{\mu}$ | Particle Reynolds number | — |
| $\displaystyle G_s = C \cdot v$ | Solids flux (Kynch) | kg/(m²·s) |
| $\displaystyle A = \frac{F}{C_c \cdot v_c}$ | Thickener area (Coe–Clevenger) | m² |
| $\displaystyle A = \frac{F \cdot t_u}{H_u \cdot \rho_s}$ | Thickener area (Kynch / Talmage–Fitch) | m² |

Where $G_s$ = solids flux, $C$ = solids concentration by volume, $F$ = solids feed rate (kg/s), $C_c$ = critical concentration, $v_c$ = settling velocity at $C_c$, $t_u$ = time to reach underflow concentration, $H_u$ = height equivalent to underflow solids, $\rho_s$ = particle density.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reynolds Number Regime Selection (KTU favourite):**
> - $Re_p \le 0.1$ → **Stokes' law** (laminar flow, fine particles).
> - $0.1 < Re_p \le 1000$ → **Intermediate (transition) regime**, use empirical drag coefficient $C_D = 18.5/Re_p^{0.6}$.
> - $Re_p \ge 1000$ → **Newton's law** (turbulent, large particles).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Jigging:** Pre-concentration of tin, tungsten, iron, gold ores; coal washing; removing dense impurities from industrial minerals.
- **Wilfley Table:** Final cleaning of tin, tungsten, chromite, ilmenite, and lead concentrates; producing high-grade metallurgical coal.
- **Continuous Thickener:** Counter-current decantation circuits in hydrometallurgy, alumina (Bayer process), copper, and uranium extraction; wastewater treatment plants; red mud separation in alumina refineries.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation 1 below is examiner-grade and frequently asked for full 14-mark derivation questions in KTU ESE.**

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stokes' Law (Terminal Settling Velocity)

**Physical Setup:**
A spherical particle of diameter $d$ and density $\rho_s$ falls under gravity through a fluid of density $\rho_f$ and viscosity $\mu$. The particle quickly reaches a **terminal velocity** $v_t$ where the gravitational force is balanced by the drag force and buoyancy.

**Step 1: Identify the three forces acting on the falling particle.**

**Gravitational force (downward):**

$$F_g = m \cdot g = \frac{\pi}{6} \, d^3 \, \rho_s \, g$$

**Buoyancy force (upward):**

$$F_b = \frac{\pi}{6} \, d^3 \, \rho_f \, g$$

**Viscous drag force (upward, by Stokes):**

$$F_d = 3 \, \pi \, \mu \, d \, v$$

**Step 2: Apply the equilibrium condition at terminal velocity.**

At terminal velocity, net force = 0:

$$F_g = F_b + F_d$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$\frac{\pi}{6} \, d^3 \, \rho_s \, g = \frac{\pi}{6} \, d^3 \, \rho_f \, g + 3 \, \pi \, \mu \, d \, v$$

**Step 3: Rearrange to isolate the viscous drag term.**

$$\frac{\pi}{6} \, d^3 \, g \, (\rho_s - \rho_f) = 3 \, \pi \, \mu \, d \, v$$

**Step 4: Divide both sides by the common factor $3 \pi d$.**

$$v = \frac{d^2 \, g \, (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{18 \, \mu}$$

**Step 5: Final Stokes' Law expression.**

$$\boxed{\,v_t = \frac{g \, d^2 \, (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{18 \, \mu}\,}$$

**Validity check:** This law is valid only when $Re_p \le 0.1$, i.e., for very small particles in viscous flow (e.g., fine silica in water).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Thickener Cross-Sectional Area (Coe & Clevenger Method)

**Step 1: Define the solids feed rate.**

The total mass of dry solids fed per unit time is:

$$F = Q_f \cdot C_f \cdot \rho_s$$

where $Q_f$ = volumetric feed flow rate (m³/s), $C_f$ = solids volume fraction in feed.

**Step 2: Recognize the limiting flux condition.**

The thickener must be sized so that the **downward solids flux** at every horizontal layer equals the **upward limiting flux** of liquid displaced by settling. At the critical concentration $C_c$, the settling velocity is $v_c$. The maximum handleable flux is:

$$G_s^{\text{lim}} = C_c \cdot v_c$$

**Step 3: Write the area-mass balance.**

For the entire feed to pass through the thickener cross-section:

$$A = \frac{F}{C_c \cdot v_c}$$

**Step 4: Substitute $F$ in terms of feed conditions.**

$$A = \frac{Q_f \cdot C_f \cdot \rho_s}{C_c \cdot v_c}$$

**Step 5: Final Coe–Clevenger expression.**

$$\boxed{\,A = \frac{Q_f \cdot C_f \cdot \rho_s}{C_c \cdot v_c}\,}$$

**Significance:** The diameter of the thickener is then $D = \sqrt{4A/\pi}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Kynch Sedimentation Theory (Conceptual Framework)

**Step 1: Kynch's Postulate.**

Kynch (1952) postulated that the settling velocity of a particle in a suspension depends **only on the local solids concentration** $C$ at that level — not on the history of the suspension.

$$v = f(C)$$

**Step 2: Solids Flux Definition.**

The volumetric flux of solids is:

$$G_s = C \cdot v = C \cdot f(C)$$

**Step 3: Mass Balance in a Sedimentation Column.**

Consider a control volume of unit cross-section and infinitesimal thickness $dz$:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial G_s}{\partial z} = 0$$

**Step 4: Chain rule expansion.**

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} + \frac{dG_s}{dC} \cdot \frac{\partial C}{\partial z} = 0$$

**Step 5: Identify the wave velocity.**

The discontinuity between two concentrations propagates with velocity:

$$u = \frac{dG_s}{dC}$$

This is the basis of the **Talmage–Fitch graphical construction**, used to find the critical underflow concentration and the time $t_u$ to reach it.

$$\boxed{\,u = \frac{dG_s}{dC} = \frac{d(C \cdot v)}{dC}\,}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example

**Problem:** A thickener receives a slurry feed of $Q_f = 0.02 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$ with solids concentration $C_f = 0.05$ (volume fraction). The critical concentration $C_c = 0.20$ and the corresponding settling velocity $v_c = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m/s}$. Particle density $\rho_s = 2500 \, \text{kg/m}^3$. Find the thickener area and diameter.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the solids feed rate.**

$$F = Q_f \cdot C_f \cdot \rho_s = 0.02 \times 0.05 \times 2500 = 2.5 \, \text{kg/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute the limiting flux.**

$$G_s^{\text{lim}} = C_c \cdot v_c = 0.20 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-4} = 3.0 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m/s}$$

But this is volumetric flux; convert to mass flux using $\rho_s$:

$$G_{s,\text{mass}}^{\text{lim}} = C_c \cdot v_c \cdot \rho_s = 0.20 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 2500 = 0.075 \, \text{kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}$$

**Step 3: Compute thickener area.**

$$A = \frac{F}{G_{s,\text{mass}}^{\text{lim}}} = \frac{2.5}{0.075} = 33.33 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 4: Compute diameter.**

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 33.33}{3.1416}} = \sqrt{42.44} = 6.51 \, \text{m}$$

**Final Answer:** Area ≈ **33.3 m²**; Diameter ≈ **6.5 m**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Wilfley Table Recovery

A Wilfley table processes 1000 kg/h of a tin ore feed assaying $\alpha = 1.2\%$ Sn. The concentrate weighs 75 kg/h and assays $\beta = 12.0\%$ Sn. Compute the recovery and Gaudin's efficiency if the tailing assays $t = 0.2\%$ Sn.

**Recovery:**

$$R = \frac{m_c \cdot \beta}{m_f \cdot \alpha} = \frac{75 \times 12.0}{1000 \times 1.2} = \frac{900}{1200} = 0.75 = 75\%$$

**Gaudin's Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{\beta(\alpha - t)}{\alpha(\beta - t)} = \frac{12.0(1.2 - 0.2)}{1.2(12.0 - 0.2)} = \frac{12.0 \times 1.0}{1.2 \times 11.8} = \frac{12.0}{14.16} = 0.847 = 84.7\%$$

**Final Answer:** Recovery = **75 %**; Gaudin's efficiency = **84.7 %**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
