# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

### Q1. Define a Mineral Jig and state the principle on which it operates. Mention any two operating variables.

**Model Answer:**
A mineral jig is a gravity concentration device in which a bed of ore particles resting on a perforated screen is subjected to alternate upward and downward pulsations of water. It works on the principle of **hindered settling**, by which particles stratify according to their **density** — heavy particles settle to the bottom and lighter gangue particles rise to the top. Two operating variables are **(i) stroke length (amplitude)** and **(ii) stroke frequency (number of pulsations per minute)**. *(3 marks)*

---

### Q2. Differentiate between the working principle of a Wilfley table and a mineral jig.

**Model Answer:**
A **mineral jig** uses a vertically pulsating water column and density-based hindered settling; it is best suited for **coarse particles (0.5–25 mm)** and produces a stratified bed. A **Wilfley table** uses a **horizontal asymmetric shaking motion** combined with a **lateral flowing water film** on an inclined riffled deck, separating particles into bands based on **density, size, and shape**; it is best suited for **fine particles (75 µm–6 mm)**. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Pattern]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **continuous thickener**. State the role of the rake arms. *(7 marks)*

**Q. (b)** A thickener is to handle 1500 tonnes/day of a slurry containing 8 % solids by weight. The underflow concentration is 60 % solids by weight. Calculate **(i) the solids feed rate in kg/s**, **(ii) the underflow volumetric flow rate** if the slurry density at 60 % solids is 1700 kg/m³, and **(iii) the thickener area** if the limiting solids flux is 1.2 × 10⁻³ m/s. Assume feed density ≈ 1050 kg/m³. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Question A (a) — Thickener Construction and Working

A continuous thickener is a large cylindrical tank, typically 10–100 m in diameter and 3–5 m deep, with the following components:

1. **Central feed well:** Receives the slurry; dissipates inlet momentum to prevent turbulence.
2. **Slow rotating rake arms (0.02–0.05 rpm):** Gently move settled sludge toward the central discharge cone without disturbing the sedimentation.
3. **Overflow launder** around the periphery collects clarified liquor.
4. **Underflow discharge** from the central cone pumps out thickened slurry.

**Three functional zones from top to bottom:**
- **Clarification Zone:** Free settling of the last fine particles to meet the clarity target.
- **Sedimentation Zone:** Hindered settling; solids concentration rises to the critical value $C_c$.
- **Compression Zone:** Network of particles consolidates under its own weight; rake arms break the arches and release trapped water.

**Role of rake arms:** They **(i) move sludge to the central discharge** and **(ii) aid water release in the compression zone** by gently breaking up the floc structure. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Question A (b) — Numerical

**Given:**
- Slurry feed = 1500 tonnes/day
- Feed solids = 8 % w/w; Underflow solids = 60 % w/w
- Slurry density (underflow) $\rho_{sl} = 1700$ kg/m³
- Limiting flux $G_s = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ m³ of solids/(m²·s)
- Feed density $\rho_f \approx 1050$ kg/m³

**(i) Solids feed rate $F$ in kg/s:**

$$F = 1500 \times 10^3 \, \text{kg/day} \times \frac{8}{100} \times \frac{1}{86400} \, \text{s/day}$$

$$F = 120{,}000 / 86{,}400 = 1.389 \, \text{kg/s}$$

**(ii) Underflow volumetric flow rate $Q_u$:**

Mass of slurry leaving as underflow = $F / 0.60 = 1.389 / 0.60 = 2.315$ kg/s of slurry.

$$Q_u = \frac{2.315}{1700} = 1.362 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**(iii) Thickener cross-sectional area:**

$$A = \frac{Q_u}{G_s} = \frac{1.362 \times 10^{-3}}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.135 \, \text{m}^2$$

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{1.445} = 1.20 \, \text{m}$$

**Final Answers:** $F = 1.389$ kg/s; $Q_u = 1.36 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s; $A = 1.135$ m²; $D \approx 1.20$ m. *(7 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q. (a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **working of a Wilfley shaking table**. Discuss the roles of asymmetric motion and water film. *(7 marks)*

**Q. (b)** A Wilfley table treats 2 tonnes/h of a chromite ore feed with 4.5 % Cr₂O₃. The concentrate weighs 120 kg/h and assays 38 % Cr₂O₃; the tailing assays 0.5 % Cr₂O₃. Calculate **(i) metallurgical recovery**, **(ii) Gaudin's separation efficiency**, and **(iii) concentration ratio**. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Question B (a) — Wilfley Table Working

The Wilfley table consists of an **inclined deck (0.25°–0.75°)** covered with **tapered triangular riffles** that decrease in height from the feed end to the concentrate end. The deck is driven by an **asymmetric crank mechanism** that gives a slow forward stroke and a fast return stroke. **Lateral wash water** flows down the deck from the upper side.

**Mechanism of separation:**

- A particle at rest is first dislodged by the water film and begins sliding sideways.
- The forward stroke gives it a longitudinal impulse; heavy particles (high inertia) continue forward during the return stroke and hence show a **net forward motion**.
- Light particles have low inertia and slide **sideways** with the water; they are washed off via the **side launder**.
- After many strokes, the deck shows a clear pattern: **concentrate band (front)**, **middling band (diagonal)**, **tailings band (lateral edge)**.

**Roles:**
- **Asymmetric motion** → drives heavy particles forward by differential inertia.
- **Water film** → transports light particles laterally, separating the density bands. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Question B (b) — Numerical

**Given:**
- Feed $m_f = 2000$ kg/h; Feed grade $\alpha = 4.5\%$
- Concentrate $m_c = 120$ kg/h; Concentrate grade $\beta = 38\%$
- Tailing grade $t = 0.5\%$

**(i) Metallurgical Recovery $R$:**

$$R = \frac{m_c \beta}{m_f \alpha} \times 100 = \frac{120 \times 38}{2000 \times 4.5} \times 100$$

$$R = \frac{4560}{9000} \times 100 = 50.67\%$$

**(ii) Gaudin's Separation Efficiency $\eta$:**

$$\eta = \frac{\beta(\alpha - t)}{\alpha(\beta - t)} \times 100 = \frac{38(4.5 - 0.5)}{4.5(38 - 0.5)} \times 100$$

$$\eta = \frac{38 \times 4.0}{4.5 \times 37.5} \times 100 = \frac{152}{168.75} \times 100 = 90.07\%$$

**(iii) Concentration Ratio $CR$:**

$$CR = \frac{\beta}{\alpha} = \frac{38}{4.5} = 8.44$$

**Final Answers:** Recovery = **50.67 %**; Gaudin efficiency = **90.07 %**; Concentration ratio = **8.44**. *(7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mistakes in thickener calculations:** Forgetting to convert tonnes/day → kg/s (divide by 86,400) — **loses 1 mark** minimum.
> 2. **Confusion between mass flux and volumetric flux:** Always check whether $G_s$ given in the problem is volumetric or mass-based; mixing them up gives a wrong area by a factor of $\rho_s$.
> 3. **Stokes' law outside its regime:** Using Stokes' law for $Re_p > 0.1$ is a common mistake. Always verify $Re_p$ first.
> 4. **Wilfley table — confusing feed end and discharge end:** Concentrate exits at the **discharge end** (where riffles are short/stop); tailings exit the **side launder** (wash water side).
> 5. **Jig — wrong identification of concentrate stream:** The concentrate exits **through the perforated screen** at the bottom (hutch side), not as overflow.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Mineral Jig:** Uses **hindered settling** in a pulsating water column; suitable for **coarse (0.5–25 mm)**; stratified bed → heavies bottom (concentrate) → lights top (tailing).
- **Wilfley Table:** Asymmetric shaking + lateral water film; separates **fine (75 µm–6 mm)**; produces **concentrate (front)**, **middling (diagonal)**, **tailing (side)**.
- **Continuous Thickener:** Gravity sedimentation in three zones (Clarification → Sedimentation → Compression); rakes aid compression.
- **Stokes' law:** $v = g d^2 (\rho_s - \rho_f)/(18\mu)$ — valid for $Re_p \le 0.1$.
- **Newton's law:** $v = 1.74 \sqrt{g d (\rho_s - \rho_f)/\rho_f}$ — valid for $Re_p \ge 1000$.
- **Recovery formula (universal):** $R = (m_c \beta)/(m_f \alpha) \times 100$ %.
- **Gaudin efficiency:** $\eta = \beta(\alpha - t)/[\alpha(\beta - t)] \times 100$ %.
- **Coe–Clevenger area:** $A = Q_f C_f \rho_s / (C_c v_c)$.
- **Talmage–Fitch method:** Based on Kynch's postulate; graphical construction on settling curve.
- **Jig variables to control:** stroke length, stroke frequency, water flow rate, feed rate.
- **Wilfley variables to control:** deck slope, stroke length, stroke frequency, wash water flow rate.
- **Thickener variables to control:** rake speed, feed well design, underflow pumping rate, flocculant dosage.
- **Remember:** Stokes' law assumes **laminar**, **Newton's law** assumes **turbulent**; check $Re_p$ first before choosing.
- **Concentration ratio** = (concentrate grade) / (feed grade); used as a quick KPI.
- **Typical plant values:** Jig frequency 60–300/min; Wilfley stroke 10–25 mm; Thickener depth 3–5 m.
- **For KTU 14-mark questions:** Always include a **block diagram** (mermaid-style) for the equipment, derive the governing equation, and solve the numerical with **units shown at every step**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Studies on Plate & frame filter press, Rotary drum filter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHL407 - PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Studies on Plate & frame filter press, Rotary drum filter # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Filtration** is a mechanical separation operation used to separate suspended insoluble solids from a fluid (liquid or gas) by forcing the mixture through a porous medium (filter medium/filter cloth) that retains the solid particles and allows the clear fluid (filtrate) to pass through. In a **Particle Technology Lab (PCCHL407)** context, Experiment 2 deals with two industrially dominant solid-liquid filtration devices: **A. Plate & Frame Filter Press** - It is a **batch-type pressure filter** consisting of alternating vertical plates and frames covered with filter cloth. The slurry is pumped under pressure into the hollow frames, the filtrate passes through the cloth, and the solid cake accumulates inside the frames. After the cycle, the press is opened and the cake is manually or mechanically discharged. **B. Rotary Drum Filter (Rotary Vacuum Drum Filter - RVDF)** - It is a **continuous-type vacuum filter** in which a cylindrical drum rotates partially submerged in a slurry tank. Vacuum applied internally sucks filtrate through the filter cloth, depositing a layer of cake on the drum surface. The cake is scraped off by a knife blade as the drum continues to rotate. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The lab exercise aims to study the **constructional features, working principle, operating sequence, and filtration characteristics (specific cake resistance, filter medium resistance, average specific resistance)** of both filters using slurry systems (commonly CaCO₃ or filter-aid suspensions). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **Analogy 1 — The Tea Strainer (Plate & Frame Filter Press)** Imagine you have many square frames each lined with a fine cloth (muslin). You pour a muddy mixture into each frame under pressure. Water seeps through the cloth, leaving mud trapped inside. Once dry, you open the frames like a sandwich, and out comes a clean mud "cake". A plate-and-frame press is essentially an *industrial sandwich* of many such frames squeezed together — that is why it is called a **filter press**. **Analogy 2 — The Rolling Paint Roller (Rotary Drum Filter)** Picture a paint roller dipped in a bucket of paint. As you roll it on a wall, the wet paint (cake) sticks to the roller surface, and excess paint (filtrate) drains back into the bucket. A doctor blade then scrapes off the dried paint. The Rotary Drum Filter works exactly like this — but instead of paint, it picks up a thin uniform cake of solid particles from the slurry, and vacuum sucks the liquid out from inside the roller. The drum keeps rotating, so the operation is **continuous**. ## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities (Standard SI Units) | Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit | |--------|----------|---------------| | **A** | Filtration Area | **m²** | | **ΔP** | Pressure Drop across filter | **Pa (N/m²)** | | **μ** | Viscosity of filtrate | **Pa·s** | | **V** | Volume of filtrate | **m³** | | **t** | Filtration time | **s** | | **R** | Filter resistance (cake) | **m⁻¹** | | **Rₘ** | Filter medium resistance | **m⁻¹** | | **r** | Specific cake resistance | **m/kg** | | **c** | Mass of solids/volume of filtrate | **kg/m³** | | **α** | Average specific resistance | **m/kg** | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Values:** Filtrate viscosities for water-based systems at 25 °C: μ_water ≈ **0.89 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**. Standard atmospheric pressure = **1.013 × 10⁵ Pa**. --- # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Principle of Plate & Frame Filter Press A Plate & Frame Filter Press operates in **four sequential cycles**: 1. **Filling Cycle (Slurry Pumping):** Slurry enters the hollow frame through the feed channel. Pressure (0.3–1.0 MPa typically) is applied by a positive displacement pump. 2. **Filtration Cycle:** Liquid passes through filter cloth into adjacent plates and drains out through filtrate outlets. Solids accumulate inside the frame as a compressible/incompressible cake. 3. **Washing Cycle (Optional):** Wash water is forced through the cake to recover residual mother liquor. 4. **Discharge Cycle:** The press is opened and the cake is removed manually or by an automatic plate-shaking mechanism. ### Working Logic — Why It Works (Step-by-Step): - The filter cloth (medium) has pores that are **smaller than the solid particles** but large enough to permit liquid flow. - Pressure differential **ΔP** drives the liquid (filtrate) through the cloth and accumulated cake. - As cake thickness increases, flow resistance grows — hence filtrate rate **dV/dt** declines with time. - This behavior is described by **Darcy's Law** applied to filtration. ## 2.2 Operational Principle of Rotary Drum Filter The drum surface is divided into **24–36 individual segments** (cells). Each segment is connected via a rotary valve to one of three external lines: - **Vacuum line** (for filtration, washing, dewatering zones) - **Atmospheric pressure** (for cake removal zone) - **Compressed air line** (for cake blow-off zone) ### Operating Zones (Per Revolution): | Zone | Approx. Angular Span | Action | |------|----------------------|--------| | Cake Formation (Filtering) | 0°–150° | Vacuum applied; cake deposited | | Washing (optional) | 150°–200° | Wash liquid sprayed; vacuum draws through cake | | Dewatering (Suction Drying) | 200°–280° | Continued vacuum; air displaces residual liquid | | Cake Discharge | 280°–360° | Compressed air inflates cloth; knife blade scrapes cake | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below are **examination-grade essentials**. Memorize each one with its unit and assumption. | # | Formula | Description | Units / Conditions | |---|---------|-------------|--------------------| | 1 | $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{A\,\Delta P}{\mu\,\left(R_c + R_m\right)}$ | Basic filtration rate (Darcy form) | Instantaneous | | 2 | $R_c = r \cdot \alpha \cdot \dfrac{c\,V}{A}$ | Cake resistance — variable pressure / general | $r$ specific cake resistance | | 3 | $R_c = \alpha \cdot \dfrac{c\,V}{A}$ | Cake resistance — for incompressible cake ($\alpha$ = const.) | kg/m³ filtrate conc. | | 4 | $\left(\dfrac{t}{V}\right) = \dfrac{\mu \alpha c}{2A^{2}\Delta P}\,V + \dfrac{\mu\,R_m}{A\,\Delta P}$ | **Constant Pressure Linear Form** | $\dfrac{t}{V}$ vs $V$ → straight line | | 5 | $V^{2} + 2\,V\,V_{e} = \dfrac{2A^{2}\,\Delta P}{\mu\alpha c}\,t$ | **Constant Pressure Quadratic Form** | $V_e$ = equiv. medium volume | | 6 | $V^{2} = \dfrac{2A^{2}\,\Delta P}{\mu\alpha c}\,t$ | When $R_m \to 0$ (medium neglected) | Ideal case | | 7 | $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{A^{2}\,\Delta P}{\mu\,(\alpha c V + A R_m)}$ | **Combined-rate equation** (used to find $\alpha$) | Substitute to get $t/V$ vs $V$ | | 8 | $\mu = \dfrac{2 A^{2} \Delta P \cdot \rho}{K}$ | $\rho$ density, $K$ constant | Viscosity relation | | 9 | $V_c = n \cdot A_f \cdot x$ | **Cake volume in filter press** | $n$ = no. of frames, $A_f$ = frame area, $x$ = cake thickness | | 10 | $V_f = \dfrac{V_c}{v_s - v_f}$ | **Filtrate volume per cycle** | $v_s$, $v_f$ = vol. fractions | | 11 | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{V_f \cdot n}{\tau}$ | Volume per unit time | $\tau$ = total cycle time | | 12 | $N_c = \dfrac{N_r \cdot \phi_f}{360°}$ | **Rotary drum**: Fraction of drum in filtration | $\phi_f$ = filtration angle | | 13 | $\text{Cake yield} = N_c \cdot A_c \cdot \rho_c$ | Mass of dry cake per revolution | $A_c$ = cake area | | 14 | $\text{Wash rate} = \dfrac{\text{Volume wash}}{\text{Wash time}}$ | Washing efficiency indicator | m³/s | | 15 | $R_m = \dfrac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot (dV/dt)_0}$ | Initial resistance of clean cloth | From intercept of $t/V$ vs $V$ | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Filter Type Used | Function | |----------|------------------|----------| | Sugar Refineries | Plate & Frame Press | Clarification of sugarcane juice | | Pharmaceutical | Rotary Drum + Plate & Frame | API crystal washing, drying | | Ceramic & Pigment | Rotary Vacuum Drum | Dewatering kaolin, TiO₂ slurries | | Wastewater Treatment | Plate & Frame | Sludge dewatering | | Edible Oils | Plate & Frame | Bleaching earth removal | | Mining (Mineral Processing) | Rotary Drum | Concentrate dewatering | --- # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the General Filtration Equation > [!NOTE] > This derivation is the **backbone of all PCCHL407 numerical problems**. You should be able to reproduce it on paper in an exam. ### Step 1 — Start with Darcy's Law Darcy's law states that the superficial velocity through a porous bed is proportional to the pressure drop and inversely proportional to the fluid viscosity and resistance. $$ \boxed{\dfrac{dV}{A\,dt} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu\,(R_c + R_m)}} $$ Where: - $V$ = cumulative volume of filtrate (m³) - $A$ = filter area (m²) - $\Delta P$ = pressure drop (Pa) - $\mu$ = filtrate viscosity (Pa·s) - $R_c$ = resistance due to cake (m⁻¹) - $R_m$ = resistance due to filter medium (m⁻¹) **Explanation:** The driving force $\Delta P$ pushes liquid through the cake + cloth. As cake grows, $R_c$ rises, slowing down the rate. ### Step 2 — Express Cake Resistance in Terms of Solids Mass For an **incompressible cake**, the specific cake resistance $\alpha$ (m/kg) is constant. The mass of solids deposited per unit filtrate volume is: $$ c = \dfrac{\text{Mass of dry cake}}{\text{Volume of filtrate}} \;\; \left[\dfrac{kg}{m^{3}}\right] $$ Therefore, cake resistance is: $$ R_c = \alpha \cdot \dfrac{c\,V}{A} $$ **Explanation:** As more filtrate $V$ passes through, more solids get deposited, and cake resistance grows linearly with $V$. ### Step 3 — Substitute into Darcy's Equation Replacing $R_c$: $$ \dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{A\,\Delta P}{\mu\,\left(\dfrac{\alpha c V}{A} + R_m\right)} = \dfrac{A^{2}\,\Delta P}{\mu\,(\alpha c V + A R_m)} $$ ### Step 4 — Rearrange for Integration Cross-multiply: $$ \mu\,(\alpha c V + A R_m)\,dV = A^{2}\,\Delta P\,dt $$ ### Step 5 — Integrate from $t = 0$, $V = 0$ to $t = t$, $V = V$ $$ \int_{0}^{V} \mu\,\alpha c\,V\,dV + \int_{0}^{V} \mu\,A R_m\,dV = \int_{0}^{t} A^{2}\,\Delta P\,dt $$ Performing term-wise integration: $$ \dfrac{\mu\,\alpha c\,V^{2}}{2} + \mu\,A R_m\,V = A^{2}\,\Delta P\,t $$ ### Step 6 — Divide Throughout by $V$ This produces the **linear form** ($t/V$ versus $V$): $$ \boxed{\dfrac{t}{V} = \dfrac{\mu\,\alpha\,c}{2\,A^{2}\,\Delta P}\,V + \dfrac{\mu\,R_m}{A\,\Delta P}} $$ **Explanation of Linear Form:** A plot of $t/V$ on the y-axis against $V$ on the x-axis gives a straight line with: - **Slope** $m = \dfrac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^{2} \Delta P}$ - **Intercept** $b = \dfrac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P}$ From these, **specific cake resistance $\alpha$** and **medium resistance $R_m$** can be determined experimentally — exactly what is done in the KTU lab experiment. ### Step 7 — Define Equivalent Filter-Medium Volume $V_e$ The intercept can be rewritten as a fictitious filtrate volume $V_e$: $$ b = \dfrac{\mu\,R_m}{A\,\Delta P} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; V_e = \dfrac{b}{m} \cdot \dfrac{V}{1} $$ So the quadratic form becomes: $$ V^{2} + 2 V\,V_e = \dfrac{2 A^{2} \Delta P}{\mu\,\alpha\,c}\,t $$ ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem:** A filter press has 20 frames, each 0.6 m × 0.6 m square, used to filter a CaCO₃ slurry containing 8% solids by mass. The slurry density is 1100 kg/m³, the cake solids density is 2500 kg/m³, and the filtrate density is 1000 kg/m³. Filter cake forms to a thickness of 25 mm per frame. If the total filtration cycle time (incl. washing, cleaning) is 30 minutes, calculate the throughput in m³/h of filtrate. ### Given: - Number of frames $n = 20$ - Frame size = $0.6 \times 0.6$ m ⇒ Frame area $A_f = 0.36$ m² - Cake thickness $x = 0.025$ m - Total cycle time $\tau = 30$ min = 0.5 h ### Step A — Volume of Cake Per Frame $$ V_{c,\,\text{frame}} = A_f \cdot x = 0.36 \times 0.025 = 0.009 \text{ m}^{3} $$ ### Step B — Total Cake Volume Per Cycle $$ V_c = n \cdot V_{c,\,\text{frame}} = 20 \times 0.009 = 0.18 \text{ m}^{3} $$ ### Step C — Solid Mass in Cake $$ m_s = V_c \cdot \rho_c = 0.18 \times 2500 = 450 \text{ kg} $$ ### Step D — Slurry Mass Corresponding to 450 kg Solids Since slurry has 8% solids by mass: $$ m_{\text{slurry}} = \dfrac{m_s}{0.08} = \dfrac{450}{0.08} = 5625 \text{ kg} $$ ### Step E — Filtrate Mass $$ m_{\text{filt}} = m_{\text{slurry}} - m_s = 5625 - 450 = 5175 \text{ kg} $$ ### Step F — Filtrate Volume $$ V_f = \dfrac{m_{\text{filt}}}{\rho_{\text{filt}}} = \dfrac{5175}{1000} = 5.175 \text{ m}^{3} $$ ### Step G — Throughput $$ \boxed{\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{V_f}{\tau} = \dfrac{5.175}{0.5} = 10.35 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{h}} $$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Plate & Frame vs Rotary Drum) | Parameter | Plate & Frame Filter Press | Rotary Drum Filter | |-----------|----------------------------|---------------------| | Operation Mode | **Batch** | **Continuous** | | Driving Force | **Pressure** (positive) | **Vacuum** (negative ΔP) | | ΔP Range | 0.3 – 1.0 MPa | 0.05 – 0.085 MPa | | Cake Thickness Control | High (set by frame thickness) | Limited (controlled by submersion depth) | | Cake Washing | Excellent (through-the-cake washing) | Moderate (spray wash) | | Labour | High (manual cake discharge) | Low (automatic scraping) | | Space | Floor-space demanding | Compact per unit area | | Capital Cost | Low–Medium | Higher (rotary + vacuum pump) | | Typical Cake Solids (Final) | 30 – 45% (dry) | 15 – 30% (damp) | | Best Suited For | Small batches, high solids recovery | Large continuous throughput, uniform slurry | --- # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Plate & Frame Filter Press — Functional Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR slurryIn["SLURRY INLET"] --> frame1["FRAME 1
CAKE FORMING ZONE"] frame1 --> frame2["FRAME 2
CAKE FORMING ZONE"] frame2 --> frameN["FRAME N
CAKE FORMING ZONE"] frameN --> outlet["FILTRATE OUTLET"] frame1 -.-> cloth1["FILTER CLOTH
RETAINS SOLIDS"] frame2 -.-> cloth2["FILTER CLOTH
RETAINS SOLIDS"] frameN -.-> clothN["FILTER CLOTH
RETAINS SOLIDS"] press["HYDRAULIC CLOSING
JACK"] --- frame1 press --- frame2 press --- frameN cakeDis["CAKE
DISCHARGE
AFTER PRESS OPENED"] --- frame1 cakeDis --- frame2 cakeDis --- frameN ``` ## 4.2 Rotary Drum Filter — Block-Level Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph DRUM["ROTATING DRUM SURFACE"] z1["ZONE 1 - CAKE FORMATION
Angle 0 to 150 deg
VACUUM APPLIED"] z2["ZONE 2 - WASHING
Angle 150 to 200 deg
SPRAY NOZZLES"] z3["ZONE 3 - DEWATERING
Angle 200 to 280 deg
VACUUM DRAWS AIR"] z4["ZONE 4 - DISCHARGE
Angle 280 to 360 deg
KNIFE BLADE SCRAPES"] z1 --> z2 --> z3 --> z4 --> z1 end slurry["SLURRY TANK"] --- z1 washIn["WASH WATER SUPPLY"] --- z2 scraper["DOCTOR KNIFE BLADE"] --- z4 valve["ROTARY VALVE
DISTRIBUTES VACUUM OR
COMPRESSED AIR"] --- z1 valve --- z2 valve --- z3 valve --- z4 filtOut["FILTRATE TO VACUUM
RECEIVER"] --- valve cakeOut["WET CAKE COLLECTED"] --- scraper ``` ## 4.3 End-to-End Process Flow — Lab Setup ```mermaid flowchart LR A[STOCK SLURRY TANK
CALCIUM CARBONATE] --> B[STIRRER
KEEPS UNIFORM] B --> C[POSITIVE DISPLACEMENT
PUMP OR VACUUM PUMP] C --> D{FILTER
SELECTION} D -->|BATCH| E[PLATE AND FRAME
FILTER PRESS] D -->|CONTINUOUS| F[ROTARY DRUM
VACUUM FILTER] E --> G[FILTRATE MEASURED
BY GRADUATED CYLINDER] E --> H[CAKE WEIGHED AFTER
DISCHARGE] F --> I[FILTRATE VOLUME PER
REVOLUTION MEASURED] F --> J[CAKE THICKNESS MEASURED
WITH VERNIER CALIPER] G --> K[CALCULATE ALPHA
SPECIFIC CAKE RESISTANCE] H --> K I --> K J --> K K --> L[REPORT
ALPHA AND Rm] ``` --- # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Differentiate between cake filtration and depth filtration. State two examples for each.** > **Model Answer:** > - **Cake Filtration:** Particles larger than the pore openings of the medium are retained **on top** of the medium, forming a continuous cake which itself becomes the filtering layer. Examples: plate & frame filter press, rotary vacuum drum filter. > - **Depth Filtration:** Particles smaller than the pores are trapped **within the depth** of the filter medium (e.g., sand filter, cartridge filter). Cake filtration is the principle governing this lab experiment. (3 marks) ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **What is specific cake resistance? State its units and physical significance.** > **Model Answer:** > Specific cake resistance ($\alpha$) is defined as the resistance offered by a unit mass of cake per unit cross-sectional area of the filter. Mathematically, $\alpha = \dfrac{\Delta P\,A}{\mu\,c\,(dV/dt)}$. > - **Units:** **m/kg** > - **Significance:** It quantifies how strongly a given cake impedes filtrate flow. A higher $\alpha$ means a denser, less permeable cake. For **incompressible** cakes (e.g., sand, CaCO₃), $\alpha$ is constant; for **compressible** cakes (e.g., metal hydroxides), $\alpha$ varies with $\Delta P$. (3 marks) --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) > [KTU University Exam – June 2024 – Modified] **(a)** Derive the constant-pressure filtration equation in its linear form $t/V$ vs $V$. Clearly state all assumptions made. (7 marks) **(b)** A plate-and-frame filter press with 30 frames of size 0.4 m × 0.4 m is used to filter a slurry containing 12% solids (by mass). The density of slurry is 1200 kg/m³, the cake density is 2200 kg/m³, and the filtrate density is 1000 kg/m³. Cake thickness formed per frame is 25 mm. Total cycle time = 45 min. Calculate: - (i) Filtrate volume per cycle (m³) - (ii) Filtration rate (m³/h) - (iii) Solid cake produced per cycle (kg) (7 marks) #### Model Solution: **(a) Derivation:** (Full derivation as in Section 3.1 of these notes.) - Start from Darcy's law: $dV/dt = A\Delta P / \mu (R_c + R_m)$ - Express cake resistance: $R_c = \alpha c V / A$ for incompressible cake - Substitute and integrate to obtain: $\dfrac{t}{V} = \dfrac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^{2} \Delta P}\,V + \dfrac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P}$ - **Assumptions:** (i) Cake is **incompressible** (constant $\alpha$), (ii) $\Delta P$ is constant, (iii) Filter medium resistance $R_m$ is constant, (iv) Cake formation is uniform across filter area. (7 marks) **(b) Numerical:** Step 1 — Frame area: $A_f = 0.4 \times 0.4 = 0.16 \text{ m}^{2}$ Step 2 — Cake volume per frame: $V_{c,\,\text{frame}} = 0.16 \times 0.025 = 0.004 \text{ m}^{3}$ Step 3 — Total cake volume: $V_c = 30 \times 0.004 = 0.12 \text{ m}^{3}$ Step 4 — Solid mass in cake: $m_s = 0.12 \times 2200 = 264 \text{ kg}$ Step 5 — Slurry mass for 264 kg solids: $m_{\text{slurry}} = 264 / 0.12 = 2200 \text{ kg}$ Step 6 — Filtrate mass: $m_f = 2200 - 264 = 1936 \text{ kg}$ Step 7 — (i) Filtrate volume: $V_f = 1936 / 1000 = \mathbf{1.936 \text{ m}^{3}}$ Step 8 — (ii) Filtration rate: $Q = V_f / \tau = 1.936 / 0.75 \text{ h} = \mathbf{2.581 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{h}}$ Step 9 — (iii) Cake mass per cycle: $m_{\text{cake}} = 264 \text{ kg (dry)} + \text{residual moisture} \approx \mathbf{264 \text{ kg}}$ (7 marks) --- ### Question B (14 Marks) > [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 – Modified] **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **working of a Rotary Vacuum Drum Filter**. List any four operating zones and their functions. (7 marks) **(b)** A rotary drum filter operating at 0.5 rev/min handles a slurry of CaCO₃. 25% of the drum surface is submerged. The filter area = 4 m², the filtrate volume per revolution = 0.003 m³, and cake density = 1600 kg/m³ with 60% moisture. Calculate: - (i) Cake thickness on the drum (m) - (ii) Dry cake production per hour (kg/h) - (iii) Filtration rate per unit area (m³/m²·h) (7 marks) #### Model Solution: **(a) Diagram and Description:** A Rotary Vacuum Drum Filter consists of a drum (covered externally with filter cloth) partially submerged in a slurry tank. Internally the drum is divided into compartments each connected to a **rotary valve**. Vacuum is applied through the rotary valve in the submerged (filtering) zone; cake forms on the cloth, the filtrate passes through the cloth and exits via the valve to a vacuum receiver. As the drum rotates out of the slurry, the cake enters the **washing and dewatering zones** (still under vacuum). In the **discharge zone**, compressed air is blown inward to inflate the cloth, loosening the cake, which is scraped off by a doctor knife. (7 marks) **Four Operating Zones:** 1. Cake Formation (submerged) 2. Washing (sprayed with wash water) 3. Dewatering (vacuum pulls air through cake) 4. Cake Discharge (knife blade) **(b) Numerical:** Given: $N_r = 0.5$ rev/min, $A = 4$ m², $V_{\text{per rev}} = 0.003$ m³, $\rho_c = 1600$ kg/m³, moisture = 60%. Step 1 — Drum surface velocity (speed of cloth): $v = \pi D \times N_r$ (not needed directly here) Step 2 — Cake area per revolution: $A_c = 0.25 \times A = 0.25 \times 4 = 1.0 \text{ m}^{2}$ (submerged area) Step 3 — (i) Cake volume per revolution: $V_c = A_c \times x$ ⇒ $x = V_c / A_c$ Wet cake volume fraction = solids + liquid; using the moisture content given: Filtrate volume per rev $V_f = 0.003 \text{ m}^{3}$. Solid mass in cake per rev $m_s = \rho_c \cdot V_c \cdot (1 - 0.6) = 0.4 \rho_c V_c$. Using material balance: $m_s = c \cdot V_f$, where $c$ = kg solids per m³ filtrate. Assume wet cake formed has volume such that its mass = filtrate deposited solids mass. Using the simplification (typical for KTU): $V_c \approx V_f / (1 - \varepsilon)$ — but here use approximate: $x = \dfrac{0.003}{1.0} = 0.003 \text{ m} = \mathbf{3 \text{ mm}}$ (1 mark) Step 4 — (ii) Cake mass per revolution (wet): $m_c = \rho_c \cdot V_c = 1600 \times 0.003 = 4.8 \text{ kg/rev}$ Revolutions per hour = $0.5 \times 60 = 30$ Wet cake/h = $4.8 \times 30 = 144 \text{ kg/h}$ Dry cake/h (40% solids in cake) = $144 \times 0.40 = \mathbf{57.6 \text{ kg/h}}$ (3 marks) Step 5 — (iii) Filtration rate per unit area: Total filtrate per hour = $0.003 \times 30 = 0.09 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{h}$ Per unit area = $0.09 / 4 = \mathbf{0.0225 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{m}^{2}\cdot\text{h}}$ (3 marks) --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cause Mark Loss:** > 1. **Mixing of mass and volume fractions:** Many students use volume fraction while the problem gives mass fraction. Always convert using densities: $w_s = \dfrac{\rho_s\,v_s}{\rho_s\,v_s + \rho_f\,v_f}$. > 2. **Forgetting to convert minutes to hours** when computing throughput — a single such error invalidates the entire numerical answer. > 3. **Substituting $\alpha$ and $c$ incorrectly:** $c$ is mass of **dry** solids per unit filtrate volume, not per unit slurry. > 4. **Confusing cake volume with filtrate volume** in filter-press calculations. > 5. **Sketching rotary drum without labeling zones** — KTU examiners specifically deduct 1–2 marks for unlabelled diagrams. > 6. **Missing assumption statements** in derivations (incompressible cake, constant $\Delta P$, uniform cake). Always state them explicitly. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Filtration** = separation of solids from fluid using a porous medium. - [x] **Plate & Frame Filter Press** is **batch, pressure-driven**; **Rotary Drum Filter** is **continuous, vacuum-driven**. - [x] Linear equation: $\dfrac{t}{V} = \dfrac{\mu\,\alpha\,c}{2A^{2}\Delta P}\,V + \dfrac{\mu R_m}{A\Delta P}$ — the slope gives $\alpha$, intercept gives $R_m$. - [x] **Specific cake resistance $\alpha$** has units **m/kg**. - [x] For **incompressible** cakes, $\alpha$ is **independent of $\Delta P$**. - [x] Rotary drum filter has **4 zones**: filtration → washing → dewatering → discharge. - [x] **Rotary valve** distributes vacuum or compressed air to each compartment as it rotates. - [x] **Filter media resistance** $R_m$ is constant throughout a run, while cake resistance grows linearly with $V$. - [x] Throughput of filter press = (filtrate volume per cycle) ÷ (cycle time). - [x] Rotary drum: submergence fraction + drum speed determine cake thickness & mass throughput. - [x] In numericals, **always distinguish** between cake solids density, slurry density, and filtrate density. - [x] For filter press sizing: $V_c = n \cdot A_f \cdot x$. - [x] Washing in filter press is **through-the-cake**, so it's highly efficient — an exam-favourite point. - [x] **Darcy's Law** is the universal starting point for all filtration derivations. - [x] If $\Delta P$ is **variable** with time, the integrated form differs — but this is generally **not asked** in Module-2 KTU lab experiments (which use constant ΔP). - [x] **Final report** in lab should plot $t/V$ vs $V$ graph and tabulate $\alpha$, $R_m$ at each concentration. - [x] KTU most-frequently asked concept: *"Why is $t/V$ plotted against $V$ and not $V$ vs $t$?"* — Because the linearized form allows graphical determination of $\alpha$ and $R_m$.

topics.title: Studies on Jaw crusher and Hammer mill
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL407 - PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Studies on Jaw crusher and Hammer mill

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Jaw Crusher — Formal Definition

A **Jaw Crusher** is a primary size-reduction equipment that operates on the **compression principle**, where a moving jaw (called the *swing jaw* or *pitman*) exerts a compressive force against a fixed jaw (called the *stationary jaw* or *toggle plate*). The material fed between the two jaws is crushed when the moving jaw approaches the fixed jaw, and is discharged through the bottom opening when the jaw recedes.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**
> The jaw crusher is classified as a **primary crusher** in the *compressive crushing* category. The most common type tested in the KTU lab is the **Blake Jaw Crusher**, which has a fixed jaw and a movable jaw pivoted at the top.

### Types of Jaw Crushers (KTU Board Focus)

| Type | Pivot Point | Throw | Application |
|------|-------------|-------|-------------|
| **Blake** | Top | Small, uniform | Hard, tough rocks — most common |
| **Dodge** | Bottom | Larger, top | Produces uniform product (lab scale) |
| **Universal** | Middle | Variable | General purpose |

---

## 1.2 Hammer Mill — Formal Definition

A **Hammer Mill** is an impact-type size-reduction machine in which a set of **high-speed rotating hammers** (mounted on a rotor) strikes the feed material. The particle shatters on impact and is also disintegrated by **attrition** against the screen/breaker plates. The crushed material exits the mill only when its size is smaller than the **aperture of the screen** fitted around the rotating assembly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**
> The hammer mill is a **secondary/tertiary crusher** operating on the **impact principle** (NOT compression). It is the most common grinder used in the KTU lab for **moderately hard to soft, non-abrasive** materials.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogies (First-Time Reader Friendly)

### 🗜️ Jaw Crusher Analogy — "The Nutcracker"
Imagine a **nutcracker** held in your hand. You place a walnut at the bottom and slowly squeeze. As the upper jaw comes down, the walnut cracks open. Now imagine this nutcracker is huge, mechanically driven by a **flywheel + eccentric shaft**, and you feed *hundreds of walnuts per minute*. That is exactly how a **Blake Jaw Crusher** operates — the moving jaw is the upper arm, the fixed jaw is the lower bed, and the **toggle plate** is the pivot that converts the rotating motion of the eccentric shaft into a squeezing (compressive) action.

### 🔨 Hammer Mill Analogy — "The Tennis Ball Cannon"
Picture a **rotating fan blade hitting tennis balls** at high speed. Each ball shatters on impact. The shattered fragments are flung outward against a **perforated wall (the screen)**, and only the small pieces that fit through the holes escape. The hammer mill works on the same principle — **high-speed impact** + **screen restriction** = fine product.

### Why Two Different Crushers? (KTU Conceptual Hook)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern**
> Examiners love to ask: *"Why use a Jaw Crusher for some materials and a Hammer Mill for others?"*
> - **Jaw Crusher** → used as a **first step** (primary) to break *very large, hard lumps* (e.g., limestone, ore, coal lumps).
> - **Hammer Mill** → used to break *already-small, soft to medium-hard* material (e.g., grains, chemicals, sugar) into **fine powder**.
> - A jaw crusher gives a **coarse product** (3 mm to 150 mm); a hammer mill gives a **fine product** (down to 100 µm).

---

## 1.4 Key Physical Quantities (Bold Constants)

The following quantities are **mandatory definitions** in KTU 2024 viva questions:

- **Reduction Ratio (RR):** $RR = \dfrac{D_{f}}{D_{p}}$ — ratio of feed particle size to product particle size.
- **Gape (G):** The maximum feed-size opening, i.e., the maximum size of lump the crusher can accept.
- **Set (S):** The minimum discharge opening, i.e., the smallest size the machine can produce.
- **Throw (s):** The vertical stroke (eccentric displacement) of the swing jaw.
- **Angle of Nip (α):** The angle between the two jaws, beyond which the particle slips without being crushed. Must satisfy **α < 2 × tan⁻¹ (µ)** where µ is the coefficient of friction between particle and jaw.
- **Critical Speed (N_c):** The maximum RPM above which the hammer mill loses impact efficiency (sleeve bearing damage).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Jaw Crusher — Constructional & Operational Details

### Components (KTU Viva List)

1. **Stationary Jaw** — fixed to the frame; lined with manganese steel plates.
2. **Swing Jaw (Pitman)** — pivoted at the top; moves up and down via the eccentric shaft.
3. **Toggle Plates** — two safety plates that convert eccentric rotation into linear jaw motion.
4. **Eccentric Shaft + Flywheel** — flywheel stores energy during the empty stroke and releases it during the crushing stroke.
5. **Toggle Seats** — support the toggle plates.
6. **Discharge Opening** — adjustable using a **shim** or **hydraulic toggle** system.

### Working Cycle (Two Strokes)

- **Crushing Stroke (Forward):** Moving jaw approaches the fixed jaw → material is compressed.
- **Idle/Retract Stroke (Backward):** Moving jaw recedes → material falls by gravity → waste is discharged.

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Storage Insight:** The flywheel absorbs energy during the idle stroke and releases it during the crushing stroke, smoothing the power demand. This is why a Blake crusher needs a **large flywheel**.

### Angle of Nip Derivation (Intuition)

The maximum angle at which a particle can be nipped between the two jaws without slipping is given by:

$$\alpha \le 2 \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{s}{2G}\right) \approx 2 \tan^{-1}(\mu)$$

For practical design, **α is kept between 17° and 30°**. If α > 30°, the particle slides out without crushing.

### Critical Operating Equation — Capacity (Taggart Equation)

The theoretical capacity of a jaw crusher (in tonnes/hour) is given by:

$$Q_t = 0.6 \times N \times s \times L \times \left(2G + S\right)$$

where:
- $N$ = RPM of eccentric shaft
- $s$ = throw (m)
- $L$ = length of receiving opening (m)
- $G$ = gape (m)
- $S$ = set (m)

> The **actual capacity** is $Q_a = K_c \times Q_t$, where $K_c$ is the compaction factor (typically **0.4 to 0.75**).

### Power Required for a Jaw Crusher

$$P = \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} \cdot s^2 \cdot \omega^2 \quad \text{(gross mechanical power)}$$

In practice, **Kick's, Rittinger's, or Bond's law** (see Section 3) is used to compute the energy-per-tonne required to reduce a given feed size to a given product size.

---

## 2.2 Hammer Mill — Constructional & Operational Details

### Components (KTU Viva List)

1. **Rotor** — central rotating shaft carrying the hammers.
2. **Hammers** — usually 4 to 12 swinging (or fixed) blades made of high-carbon steel, pivoted on the rotor.
3. **Breaker Plate (Liner)** — stationary plate that helps shatter particles on rebound.
4. **Screen / Grating** — perforated plate that controls the maximum product size.
5. **Hopper / Feeder** — usually gravity-fed; can be vibratory for controlled feed.
6. **Cyclone / Bag Filter (downstream)** — separates product from air stream.

### Working Principle (Three-Stage Process)

1. **Impact** — Hammers strike the particle at high velocity.
2. **Attrition** — Particles rub against each other, the screen, and the breaker plate.
3. **Classification** — Only particles smaller than the screen aperture leave the mill.

### Critical Speed of Hammer Mill

The rotor must rotate below the **critical speed** to prevent failure. For a rotor of mass *M* mounted on a flexible shaft:

$$N_c = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{M}}$$

where $k$ is the stiffness of the shaft. Operating speed is typically **60–80%** of $N_c$.

### Capacity of Hammer Mill (Approximate)

$$\dot{m}_{hammer} = K_h \cdot A_{screen} \cdot \rho_{bulk} \cdot D_{screen} \cdot N$$

where:
- $K_h$ = empirical constant (≈ 0.1)
- $A_{screen}$ = effective screen area
- $\rho_{bulk}$ = bulk density of feed
- $D_{screen}$ = screen aperture diameter
- $N$ = rotor RPM

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Law / Equation | Mathematical Form | Best For | Constant |
|---|----------------|-------------------|----------|----------|
| 1 | **Reduction Ratio** | $RR = \dfrac{D_f}{D_p}$ | All crushers | — |
| 2 | **Angle of Nip** | $\alpha \le 2\tan^{-1}(\mu)$ | Jaw crusher design | $\mu$ = friction coeff. |
| 3 | **Taggart Capacity** | $Q_t = 0.6\,N\,s\,L\,(2G+S)$ | Jaw crusher | $K_c = 0.4\text{–}0.75$ |
| 4 | **Kick's Law (1885)** | $E = K_K \ln\!\left(\dfrac{D_f}{D_p}\right)$ | Coarse crushing, jaw crusher | $K_K$ in kWh/tonne |
| 5 | **Rittinger's Law (1867)** | $E = K_R\!\left(\dfrac{1}{D_p} - \dfrac{1}{D_f}\right)$ | Fine grinding, hammer mill | $K_R$ in kWh·m/tonne |
| 6 | **Bond's Law (1952)** | $E = K_B\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_p}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_f}}\right)$ | Intermediate size reduction | $K_B = 2W_i$ (Work Index) |
| 7 | **Rittinger Surface-Area Form** | $E = K_R\,(S_p - S_f)$ | New surface created | $S$ = specific surface |
| 8 | **Bond Work Index Eqn.** | $W = W_i \!\left(\dfrac{10}{\sqrt{D_p}} - \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{D_f}}\right)$ | Power sizing of mills | $W_i$ in kWh/tonne |
| 9 | **Power Required** | $P = \dot{m} \cdot E$ | Equipment motor sizing | $P$ in kW |
| 10 | **Hammer Tip Speed** | $v_{tip} = \pi D_{rotor} N$ | Impact energy estimation | $v_{tip} = 30\text{–}100$ m/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip — Law Selection**
> - If the question says **"coarse crushing / jaw crusher"** → use **Kick's Law**.
> - If the question says **"fine grinding / hammer mill / new surface"** → use **Rittinger's Law**.
> - If the question says **"intermediate / general purpose / work index given"** → use **Bond's Law**.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Equipment | Industrial Use |
|-----------|----------------|
| **Jaw Crusher** | Cement industry (limestone primary crushing), mining (ore preparation), road ballast production, mineral benefaction |
| **Hammer Mill** | Pharmaceutical (API milling), food (flour, sugar, spices), animal feed, biomass pellet preparation, fertilizer grinding |
| **Combined Use** | Cement plants: Jaw crusher (primary) → Hammer mill (secondary) → Ball mill (fine) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Kick's Law (Volume Theory)

**Premise:** The energy required for size reduction is **proportional to the ratio of the initial volume to the final volume** of the particle. The strain energy per unit mass is the integral of stress with respect to strain.

**Step 1:** Consider a cube of side $D_f$. The work done per unit mass to deform and break it scales with the *change in volume* (geometry):

$$dE = \frac{\sigma}{\rho} \cdot d\varepsilon$$

where $\sigma$ is the crushing stress and $\varepsilon$ is the strain (≈ volumetric deformation).

**Step 2:** For elastic deformation up to fracture, assuming Hookean behaviour:

$$\sigma = E_m \cdot \varepsilon$$

where $E_m$ is the elastic modulus. Substituting:

$$dE = \frac{E_m}{\rho} \cdot \varepsilon \, d\varepsilon$$

**Step 3:** Integrating from $0$ to the fracture strain $\varepsilon_f$:

$$E = \frac{E_m}{2\rho} \cdot \varepsilon_f^2$$

**Step 4:** The fracture strain is proportional to the **logarithm of the size ratio** (because strain in a crushing geometry = ln($D_f/D_p$)):

$$\varepsilon_f \propto \ln\!\left(\frac{D_f}{D_p}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Substituting and collecting all material-dependent terms into a single constant $K_K$:

$$\boxed{E = K_K \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{D_f}{D_p}\right)}$$

This is **Kick's Law**, valid for *coarse crushing where deformation dominates over new surface creation*.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Rittinger's Law (Surface-Area Theory)

**Premise:** The energy required for size reduction is **proportional to the new surface area created** (since breaking bonds requires surface energy).

**Step 1:** Let the feed particle be a cube of side $D_f$. Its surface area is:

$$A_f = 6 D_f^2$$

**Step 2:** After crushing into $N$ smaller cubes of side $D_p$, the total surface area is:

$$A_p = N \cdot 6 D_p^2$$

**Step 3:** Mass conservation (density $\rho$ unchanged) gives:

$$N \cdot D_p^3 = D_f^3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N = \left(\frac{D_f}{D_p}\right)^3$$

**Step 4:** New surface created per unit mass is:

$$\Delta A = \frac{A_p - A_f}{\rho D_f^3} = \frac{6 D_p^2 \left(\dfrac{D_f}{D_p}\right)^3 - 6 D_f^2}{\rho D_f^3} = \frac{6}{\rho}\!\left(\frac{1}{D_p} - \frac{1}{D_f}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Energy is proportional to new surface ($\gamma$ = surface energy per unit area, a material constant):

$$E = \gamma \cdot \Delta A = \frac{6\gamma}{\rho} \left(\frac{1}{D_p} - \frac{1}{D_f}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Combining all material-dependent terms into a single constant $K_R$:

$$\boxed{E = K_R \left(\frac{1}{D_p} - \frac{1}{D_f}\right)}$$

This is **Rittinger's Law**, valid for *fine grinding where new surface area dominates energy consumption*.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Bond's Law (Third Theory)

**Premise:** Energy is proportional to the **arithmetic mean** of Kick's and Rittinger's energy forms — a "compromise" law.

**Step 1:** Take the average of Kick's and Rittinger's energies (with equal weight):

$$E_{Bond} = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(E_{Kick} + E_{Rittinger}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\!\left[K_K \ln\!\frac{D_f}{D_p} + K_R\!\left(\frac{1}{D_p} - \frac{1}{D_f}\right)\right]$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate with respect to $D_p$ (Bond's key insight — derivative of energy w.r.t. size is constant per unit length, mirroring the **crack-propagation theory** of Griffith):

$$\frac{dE_{Bond}}{dD_p} = -\frac{1}{D_p} \cdot f(D_p)$$

**Step 3:** Bond postulated that the **differential energy per unit size** is constant, which gives an inverse-square-root dependence:

$$\frac{dE}{dD} \propto \frac{1}{D^{3/2}}$$

**Step 4:** Integrating:

$$E = K_B \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_p}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_f}}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Bond related the constant to the **Work Index** $W_i$ (energy to reduce a theoretically infinite size to 100 µm), giving the famous working form:

$$\boxed{W = W_i \left(\frac{10}{\sqrt{D_p}} - \frac{10}{\sqrt{D_f}}\right)}$$

where $W$ is the work input (kWh/tonne), $W_i$ is Bond's Work Index, and $D$ is in **micrometres**.

---

## 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (KTU-Style)

> **Problem [KTU University Exam, June 2022 — Modified]:**
> A jaw crusher reduces limestone from a feed size of $D_f = 50$ mm to a product size of $D_p = 6$ mm. The feed rate is $\dot{m} = 50$ tonnes/hour. Using **Kick's Law** with $K_K = 3.5$ kWh/tonne, calculate the power required by the crusher motor.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Identify given data
- Feed size $D_f = 50$ mm
- Product size $D_p = 6$ mm
- Mass feed rate $\dot{m} = 50$ t/h
- Kick's constant $K_K = 3.5$ kWh/tonne

**Step 2:** Apply Kick's Law

$$E = K_K \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{D_f}{D_p}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values

$$E = 3.5 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{50}{6}\right) = 3.5 \times \ln(8.333)$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the logarithm

$$\ln(8.333) = 2.120$$

**Step 5:** Calculate energy per tonne

$$E = 3.5 \times 2.120 = 7.42 \text{ kWh/tonne}$$

**Step 6:** Calculate power required

$$P = \dot{m} \cdot E = 50 \times 7.42 = 371 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 371 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 7:** With an assumed motor efficiency of $\eta_{motor} = 0.90$, the **shaft power** is:

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{ideal}}{\eta_{motor}} = \frac{371}{0.90} = 412.2 \text{ kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Insight:** Always state the units explicitly and round to **three significant figures**. Marks are cut if students forget to multiply by $\dot{m}$ to get actual power (Step 6).

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix (When to Use Which Law)

| Parameter | Kick's Law | Rittinger's Law | Bond's Law |
|-----------|------------|------------------|-------------|
| **Year / Founder** | 1885, Kick | 1867, Rittinger | 1952, Bond |
| **Underlying Theory** | Volume strain | New surface area | Crack-propagation (geometric mean) |
| **Size Range** | Coarse (> 5 mm) | Fine (< 0.1 mm) | Intermediate (0.5–5 mm) |
| **Best Crusher** | Jaw, Gyratory | Ball mill, Hammer mill | Rod mill, Autogenous |
| **Energy Form** | $E = K_K \ln(D_f/D_p)$ | $E = K_R(1/D_p - 1/D_f)$ | $E = K_B(1/\sqrt{D_p} - 1/\sqrt{D_f})$ |
| **Constant Type** | Material strength modulus | Surface energy × particle geometry | Work Index $W_i$ |
| **KTU Typical Use** | Primary crusher calculation | Fine grinder calculation | General purpose (exam most common) |

---

## 3.6 Reduction Ratio Quick-Reference (Exam Memory)

$$\text{Jaw Crusher: } RR = 4 \text{ to } 9$$
$$\text{Hammer Mill: } RR = 20 \text{ to } 40$$
$$\text{Ball Mill: } RR = 50 \text{ to } 100$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** A common mistake is to confuse $RR$ with the **area reduction**. The reduction ratio is a *size* ratio (length), NOT an area ratio.

    I -->|alpha greater than 30 deg| K[Particle Slips Out No Crushing]

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Define Reduction Ratio. State its typical value for (a) a jaw crusher and (b) a hammer mill."**

**Model Answer:**
The **Reduction Ratio (RR)** is defined as the ratio of the maximum size of feed particle ($D_f$) to the maximum size of product particle ($D_p$):

$$RR = \frac{D_f}{D_p}$$

**(a) Jaw Crusher:** $RR$ is typically in the range of **4 : 1 to 9 : 1** (primary crushing application).
**(b) Hammer Mill:** $RR$ is typically in the range of **20 : 1 to 40 : 1** (high-speed impact grinding).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**"Distinguish between Rittinger's and Kick's law of crushing. State one application of each."**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Kick's Law (1885) | Rittinger's Law (1867) |
|-------|--------------------|-------------------------|
| **Theory** | Volume strain energy | New surface area creation |
| **Equation** | $E = K_K \ln(D_f/D_p)$ | $E = K_R(1/D_p - 1/D_f)$ |
| **Best for** | Coarse crushing | Fine grinding |
| **Application** | Jaw crusher for limestone | Hammer mill for pharmaceutical powder |

> Kick's law is used when the energy is dominated by **elastic deformation of large lumps**; Rittinger's law is used when the energy is dominated by **creation of new surface** in fine grinding.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working of a Blake Jaw Crusher**. Discuss the role of (i) flywheel and (ii) toggle plate. (7 marks)

**(b)** A jaw crusher is fed with limestone at the rate of **120 tonnes/hour**. The average size of the feed is $\mathbf{D_f = 60 \text{ mm}}$ and the average product size is $\mathbf{D_p = 8 \text{ mm}}$. Using **Kick's Law** with $\mathbf{K_K = 4.2 \text{ kWh/tonne}}$, calculate the **power required** by the crusher motor. Assume motor efficiency = 0.85. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

A **Blake Jaw Crusher** consists of:
- A **fixed (stationary) jaw** mounted on the body.
- A **moving (swing) jaw** pivoted at the top.
- An **eccentric shaft** with a heavy **flywheel** at one end.
- Two **toggle plates** connecting the moving jaw to the eccentric shaft.

**Working:**
The motor drives the eccentric shaft, which rotates. The rotation is converted into an **up-and-down reciprocating motion** of the moving jaw via the toggle plates. During the **forward (closing) stroke**, the moving jaw approaches the fixed jaw and the material in the crushing chamber is **compressed and broken**. During the **backward (retract) stroke**, the jaw moves away and the crushed material falls by gravity through the discharge opening.

**Role of Flywheel:** The flywheel **stores kinetic energy** during the retract (idle) stroke and **releases it** during the crushing (forward) stroke. This smooths out the **fluctuating power demand** from the motor and reduces peak load.

**Role of Toggle Plate:** The toggle plate (a) **transmits motion** from the eccentric shaft to the moving jaw, (b) acts as a **safety fuse** by breaking under overload conditions, and (c) provides a mechanical advantage to amplify the crushing force.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1:** Given Data
- $\dot{m} = 120$ t/h
- $D_f = 60$ mm
- $D_p = 8$ mm
- $K_K = 4.2$ kWh/t
- $\eta_{motor} = 0.85$

**Step 2:** Apply Kick's Law

$$E = K_K \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{D_f}{D_p}\right) = 4.2 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{60}{8}\right) = 4.2 \times \ln(7.5)$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate $\ln(7.5) = 2.015$

$$E = 4.2 \times 2.015 = 8.46 \text{ kWh/tonne}$$

**Step 4:** Power required (ideal)

$$P_{ideal} = \dot{m} \cdot E = 120 \times 8.46 = 1015.2 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5:** Account for motor efficiency

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{ideal}}{\eta_{motor}} = \frac{1015.2}{0.85}$$

$$\boxed{P_{shaft} \approx 1194.4 \text{ kW} \approx 1.19 \text{ MW}}$$

---

### Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**(a)** Explain the **construction and working of a Hammer Mill** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the **role of the screen** in determining product size. (7 marks)

**(b)** A hammer mill is to grind **coal** from a feed size of $\mathbf{D_f = 4 \text{ mm}}$ to a product size of $\mathbf{D_p = 0.25 \text{ mm}}$. The feed rate is $\mathbf{\dot{m} = 4 \text{ tonnes/hour}}$. Using **Rittinger's Law** with $\mathbf{K_R = 200 \text{ kWh·mm/tonne}}$, calculate: (i) the **energy required per tonne** and (ii) the **power required**. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

A **Hammer Mill** consists of:
- A **hopper** at the top for feeding.
- A **rotor** carrying a series of **hammers** (4 to 12) pivoted on pin joints.
- A **breaker plate (liner)** on one side.
- A **perforated screen / grating** covering the bottom and lower sides of the mill.
- A **drive system** (motor + V-belt + pulley).

**Working:**
The motor drives the rotor at high speed (typically 1500 to 4000 RPM). The hammers swing outward due to centrifugal force and strike the feed particles with high velocity (**impact**). The particles shatter and are thrown against the breaker plate, where they undergo further **attrition**. The broken fragments fall toward the screen, and only those **smaller than the screen aperture** can exit the mill. Oversized particles are retained and undergo further impact/attrition until they pass.

**Role of the Screen:**
The screen acts as a **size classifier** at the discharge. The **maximum product size** is **directly controlled by the screen aperture** — a finer screen gives a finer product, a coarser screen gives a coarser product. The screen also provides additional attrition because retained particles are forced to slide against it.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1:** Given Data
- $D_f = 4$ mm
- $D_p = 0.25$ mm
- $\dot{m} = 4$ t/h
- $K_R = 200$ kWh·mm/tonne

**Step 2:** Apply Rittinger's Law

$$E = K_R \left(\frac{1}{D_p} - \frac{1}{D_f}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Compute the reciprocals

$$\frac{1}{D_p} = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4.0 \text{ mm}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{D_f} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 \text{ mm}^{-1}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute

$$E = 200 \times (4.0 - 0.25) = 200 \times 3.75 = 750 \text{ kWh/tonne}$$

**Step 5:** Power required

$$P = \dot{m} \cdot E = 4 \times 750 = 3000 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{E = 750 \text{ kWh/t}, \quad P = 3000 \text{ kW} = 3.0 \text{ MW}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Note:** This high power is typical for *very fine* grinding. In practice, the feed rate would be drastically reduced, or multiple parallel mills would be used, to match a real-world motor rating.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (KTU 2018-2024 Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Wrong Law Used:** Students apply Rittinger's law for a jaw crusher (coarse) or Kick's law for a ball mill (fine). *Always match the law to the equipment.*
> 2. **Forgotten Unit Conversion:** Some KTU questions give $D$ in cm and expect the answer in m. *Check units of $K_R$ and $D$ match.*
> 3. **Missing Factor of $\dot{m}$:** Many students compute $E$ (energy per tonne) but forget to multiply by $\dot{m}$ to get the **actual power** $P$ in kW. The question asks for "power required" → you MUST multiply by mass flow rate.
> 4. **No Efficiency Term:** Real-world motor efficiency ($\eta \approx 0.85$–$0.90$) is usually demanded in Part B. Forgetting it costs **1–2 marks**.
> 5. **Angle of Nip Definition:** Many students write $\alpha \le 2\mu$ instead of $\alpha \le 2\tan^{-1}(\mu)$. The tangent function is mandatory.
> 6. **Direction of Ratio:** Mixing up $D_f/D_p$ vs $D_p/D_f$ — Kick's law uses the ratio **feed/product** (i.e., $D_f/D_p$). Inverting it gives a negative energy. *Always check sign.*
> 7. **Hammer Mill Confused with Ball Mill:** Both produce fine product, but a hammer mill is *impact* and a ball mill is *compression + impact*. Examiners deduct marks if you interchange their principles.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Jaw Crusher:**
> - ☐ Primary crusher; compressive action; Blake / Dodge / Universal types.
> - ☐ Reduction Ratio = $D_f / D_p$ (typical **4 to 9**).
> - ☐ Components: fixed jaw, swing jaw, toggle plate, eccentric shaft, flywheel.
> - ☐ Angle of Nip: $\alpha \le 2 \tan^{-1}(\mu)$ — keep **17° to 30°**.
> - ☐ Taggart capacity: $Q_t = 0.6 N s L (2G + S)$.
> - ☐ Energy law used: **Kick's Law** ($E = K_K \ln(D_f/D_p)$).

> **Hammer Mill:**
> - ☐ Impact + attrition; not compressive.
> - ☐ Reduction Ratio = $D_f / D_p$ (typical **20 to 40**).
> - ☐ Components: rotor, hammers (4–12), breaker plate, screen, hopper.
> - ☐ Product size is **screen-controlled**.
> - ☐ Energy law used: **Rittinger's Law** ($E = K_R (1/D_p - 1/D_f)$).
> - ☐ Tip speed: $v_{tip} = \pi D_{rotor} N$ (range 30–100 m/s).

> **Energy Laws — Master Memory:**
> - ☐ **Kick (1885)** → Volume / Coarse → use $\ln(D_f/D_p)$.
> - ☐ **Rittinger (1867)** → Surface / Fine → use $(1/D_p - 1/D_f)$.
> - ☐ **Bond (1952)** → Work Index / Intermediate → use $W = W_i(10/\sqrt{D_p} - 10/\sqrt{D_f})$ where $D$ in µm.
> - ☐ Always remember: $P = \dot{m} \cdot E$ (kW = t/h × kWh/t).

> **Numerical Answer Format (KTU Board):**
> - ☐ State the given data clearly.
> - ☐ State the law being used and the reason.
> - ☐ Show the equation, substitute values, evaluate, and **box the final answer** with units.
> - ☐ Mention the **significant figures** (typically 3 SF in KTU papers).

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Experiment Selected: Determination of Particle Size Distribution by Sedimentation Method (Andreasen Pipette)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> *Sedimentation analysis* is a **gravitational settling technique** used to determine the **particle size distribution of fine powders** (typically below 75 μm) that cannot be resolved by standard sieve analysis. The *Andreasen Pipette* method is a **liquid sedimentation procedure** based on **Stokes' Law**, in which the concentration of suspended solid particles remaining at a fixed depth below the suspension surface is measured as a function of settling time. The cumulative mass of particles of diameter greater than a calculated Stokes diameter is then determined to construct the particle size distribution curve.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Muddy River & The Diving Bell":**
> Imagine pouring a bucket of muddy water (a suspension of fine soil particles) into a tall, transparent glass cylinder. The **heaviest, biggest grains** of mud fall to the bottom almost instantly — like boulders tumbling down a hill. The **tiny, light grains** stay suspended for a long time, almost as if they are floating in the air. If you place a small sampling tube (the *Andreasen pipette*) at a specific depth, say 10 cm below the surface, and withdraw a tiny sample **after a calculated time interval**, you are effectively capturing **only the particles that were too small to have settled past that depth in that time**.
> - Particles **larger** than a certain size have already fallen *below* the sampling point — they are missed.
> - Particles **smaller** than that size are *still floating* at that depth — they are captured.
> - By repeating this at different time intervals, you effectively "weigh" how many particles of each size family remain in the cloud. This gives the **size distribution**.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Constants (Bolded)

- **Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 9.81 \ \mathrm{m/s^2}$** (standard value for KTU lab calculations).
- **Density of water at laboratory temperature** (typically 30 °C): $\rho_f \approx 995.6 \ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$.
- **Stokes' Law Validity Range — Particle Reynolds Number, $Re_p < 0.2$** (also called the *Stokes range*).
- **Sample withdrawal depth** in Andreasen Pipette = typically $h = 0.20 \ \mathrm{m}$ (20 cm).
- **Suspension concentration** typically prepared at $1\%$ w/v or $10 \ \mathrm{g}$ solid in $1000 \ \mathrm{cm^3}$ of water (after correction for added dispersant).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment is the standard *"any other experiment on particle size analysis"* for the KTU 2024 Scheme PCCHL407 lab. It directly tests **CO2 (Apply particle size distribution and reduction principles)**, **CO3 (Demonstrate size analysis using standard apparatus)**, and **CO4 (Analyse data to obtain size distribution)**.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The Andreasen Pipette method is built on three coupled physical principles:

1. **Stokes' Law (Terminal Settling Velocity):**
   When a single spherical particle settles under gravity in a viscous fluid at very low Reynolds number, viscous drag balances the net gravitational (buoyancy-corrected) weight, and the particle attains a constant *terminal velocity*.

2. **Hindered Settling Negligibility:**
   The suspension must be **sufficiently dilute** (typically $\leq 1\%$ by volume) so that particle-particle interactions do not alter the settling velocity. Andreasen recommended a concentration of about **0.5–1.0 % v/v** for this reason.

3. **Sampling at Fixed Depth = Particle Diameter Selection:**
   By withdrawing a sample from a *known depth* $h$ below the surface after a *known time* $t$, the operator effectively selects only those particles whose **Stokes diameter $D$** is **less than or equal to the diameter that would have just settled through that depth in that time**.

### 2.2 Derivation of the Working Diameter Equation

Stokes' law gives the terminal settling velocity:

$$v_t = \frac{h}{t}$$

Equating the two expressions for terminal velocity:

$$\frac{h}{t} = \frac{D^2 \, g \, (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \, \mu}$$

Rearranging for the **Stokes diameter $D$**:

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{18 \, \mu \, h}{g \, (\rho_p - \rho_f) \, t}}$$

where:
- $D$ = Stokes diameter of the particle (m)
- $h$ = depth of sampling below suspension surface (m)
- $t$ = elapsed settling time (s)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $\rho_p$ = density of the solid particle (kg/m³)
- $\rho_f$ = density of the fluid (kg/m³)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of the fluid (Pa·s or kg/m·s)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol | Units | Boundary / Validity Condition |
|---|---------------------|--------|-------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | Terminal settling velocity (Stokes) | $v_t = \dfrac{D^2 g (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \mu}$ | m/s | $Re_p \le 0.2$ (laminar, no inertia) |
| 2 | Stokes diameter (Andreasen) | $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{18 \mu h}{g (\rho_p - \rho_f) t}}$ | m | Dilute suspension; $\le 1\%$ v/v |
| 3 | Particle Reynolds number | $Re_p = \dfrac{D \, v_t \, \rho_f}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Must be $\le 0.2$ |
| 4 | Cumulative % undersize (mass) | $M(t) = \dfrac{C(t)}{C_0} \times 100$ | % | $C(t)$ = concentration at depth $h$ at time $t$ |
| 5 | Cumulative % oversize | $100 - M(t)$ | % | Plotted vs $D$ on log scale |
| 6 | Mass of sample withdrawn | $m = V_s \times C(t)$ | g | $V_s$ = pipette volume (typically 10 cm³) |
| 7 | Average diameter for differential plot | $D_{avg} = \dfrac{D_1 + D_2}{2}$ | m | Between two consecutive sampling times |
| 8 | Differential mass % (between sizes) | $\Delta M = M(t_1) - M(t_2)$ | % | $t_1 < t_2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Pitfall:** The pipe symbol $\mid$ in the Reynolds number or absolute value expressions must NEVER be written as a raw `|` character inside a markdown table — use `\vert` or `\mid` instead to avoid breaking table rendering. Always check: $Re_p \le 0.2$ validates Stokes' law applicability for *each* calculated diameter.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ceramic and cement industries:** Quality control of raw meal fineness (sub-sieve range).
- **Pharmaceutical powder processing:** Ensuring uniform drug particle size for predictable dissolution.
- **Paint and pigment manufacture:** Particle size governs tinting strength, gloss and rheology.
- **Soil mechanics and geotechnical engineering:** Classification of clays and silts.
- **Food industry (cocoa, flour, milk powder):** Texture and flow behaviour control.
- **Catalyst manufacturing:** Active surface area is inversely related to particle size.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Analysis

### 3.1 Derivation of the Stokes Diameter Equation (Exhaustive)

We start from the **force balance on a single spherical particle** settling at terminal velocity in a viscous fluid.

**Step 1 — Identify the three forces acting on a settling sphere:**

1. **Gravitational force (downward):**
$$F_g = \frac{\pi}{6} D^3 \rho_p g$$

2. **Buoyant force (upward):**
$$F_b = \frac{\pi}{6} D^3 \rho_f g$$

3. **Viscous drag force (Stokes' drag, upward):**
$$F_d = 3 \pi \mu D v_t$$

**Step 2 — Net downward force:**

$$F_{net} = F_g - F_b = \frac{\pi}{6} D^3 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g$$

**Step 3 — Apply terminal velocity condition ($F_{net} = F_d$):**

$$\frac{\pi}{6} D^3 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g = 3 \pi \mu D v_t$$

**Step 4 — Simplify by cancelling $\pi$ and $D$:**

$$\frac{1}{6} D^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g = 3 \mu v_t$$

$$D^2 = \frac{18 \mu v_t}{g (\rho_p - \rho_f)}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $v_t = h / t$ (terminal velocity = depth divided by elapsed time):**

$$D^2 = \frac{18 \mu h}{g (\rho_p - \rho_f) t}$$

**Step 6 — Take the square root to obtain the final working equation:**

$$\boxed{D = \sqrt{\frac{18 \mu h}{g (\rho_p - \rho_f) t}}}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU-Style)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A sample of quartz ($\rho_p = 2650 \ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$) is suspended in water ($\rho_f = 1000 \ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$, $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \ \mathrm{Pa \cdot s}$) at 30 °C in an Andreasen Pipette. The sampling depth is $h = 0.20 \ \mathrm{m}$. The initial concentration $C_0$ of the suspension corresponds to an oven-dried mass of $0.500 \ \mathrm{g}$ per $10 \ \mathrm{cm^3}$ sample. After a settling time of **$t = 600 \ \mathrm{s}$**, a 10 cm³ sample is withdrawn and the dried mass is found to be $m = 0.250 \ \mathrm{g}$. Calculate:
> 1. The Stokes diameter of the largest particle remaining in suspension at that depth.
> 2. The cumulative mass percentage of particles finer than this diameter.
> 3. The particle Reynolds number to verify Stokes' law applicability.
> 4. The cumulative mass percentage of particles **coarser** than this diameter.

#### Part (i): Stokes Diameter

Using the working equation derived above:

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{18 \times (1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.20}{9.81 \times (2650 - 1000) \times 600}}$$

Computing the numerator:

$$18 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.20 = 3.6 \times 10^{-3}$$

Computing the denominator:

$$9.81 \times 1650 \times 600 = 9.81 \times 990000 = 9.7119 \times 10^{6}$$

Therefore:

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{3.6 \times 10^{-3}}{9.7119 \times 10^{6}}} = \sqrt{3.707 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$D = 1.925 \times 10^{-5} \ \mathrm{m} = 19.25 \ \mathrm{\mu m}$$

#### Part (ii): Cumulative % Undersize (Mass Fraction Finer)

The mass of solid per 10 cm³ sample at time $t$ is $m = 0.250 \ \mathrm{g}$. The initial mass per 10 cm³ is $C_0 = 0.500 \ \mathrm{g}$. The cumulative mass percentage finer than $D$ is:

$$M(t) = \frac{m}{C_0} \times 100 = \frac{0.250}{0.500} \times 100 = 50.0 \ \mathrm{\%}$$

#### Part (iii): Particle Reynolds Number Check

First compute the terminal velocity:

$$v_t = \frac{h}{t} = \frac{0.20}{600} = 3.333 \times 10^{-4} \ \mathrm{m/s}$$

Then:

$$Re_p = \frac{D \, v_t \, \rho_f}{\mu} = \frac{1.925 \times 10^{-5} \times 3.333 \times 10^{-4} \times 1000}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$Re_p = \frac{6.416 \times 10^{-6}}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 6.42 \times 10^{-3}$$

Since $Re_p = 0.00642 \le 0.2$, **Stokes' law is valid**. ✓

#### Part (iv): Cumulative % Oversize (Coarser than 19.25 μm)

$$\text{\% Coarser} = 100 - 50.0 = 50.0 \ \mathrm{\%}$$

### 3.3 Standard Observation & Calculation Table Format (For KTU Lab Record)

| S. No. | Time $t$ (s) | Depth $h$ (m) | Mass dried $m$ (g) | $C(t) = m/V_s$ (g/cm³) | Diameter $D$ (μm) | Cum. % Finer | Cum. % Coarser |
|:------:|:------------:|:-------------:|:------------------:|:---------------------:|:-----------------:|:------------:|:--------------:|
| 1 | 60 | 0.20 | 0.460 | 0.046 | 60.9 | 92.0 | 8.0 |
| 2 | 180 | 0.20 | 0.380 | 0.038 | 34.9 | 76.0 | 24.0 |
| 3 | 600 | 0.20 | 0.250 | 0.025 | 19.3 | 50.0 | 50.0 |
| 4 | 1800 | 0.20 | 0.140 | 0.014 | 11.0 | 28.0 | 72.0 |
| 5 | 7200 | 0.20 | 0.060 | 0.006 | 5.5 | 12.0 | 88.0 |
| 6 | 21600 | 0.20 | 0.020 | 0.002 | 3.2 | 4.0 | 96.0 |

### 3.4 Graphical Plots Required in the Lab Record

1. **Cumulative % Finer (linear) vs $D$ (log scale) on x-axis** — the *S-curve*, used to read off any $D$-value.
2. **Cumulative % Finer (probability axis) vs $D$ (log scale)** — should approach a straight line if the powder is log-normally distributed.
3. **Differential frequency curve:** $\Delta M / \Delta(\log D)$ vs $\log D$ — used to identify the **mode** (most frequent size).

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of the Andreasen Pipette Apparatus

The Andreasen Pipette is a **graduated glass cylinder** (typically 500 cm³ or 550 cm³ capacity) fitted with a **side-sampling pipette** at a fixed depth (20 cm from the top) connected via a **two-way stopcock** to a **withdrawal pipette** of known volume (usually 10 cm³). The block-level functional architecture is:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Suspension Preparation Vessel: 0.5-1% v/v solid in liquid with dispersant] --> B[Transfer to Andreasen Cylinder: 500-550 cm3 graduated glass]
    B --> C[Stopclock Start t0 = 0 s]
    C --> D[Temperature Equilibration: 30 degree C water bath]
    D --> E{Sampling Time Reached?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Open Two-Way Stopcock]
    F --> G[Withdraw 10 cm3 Sample from Side Pipette at h = 0.20 m]
    G --> H[Transfer to Pre-weighed Evaporating Dish]
    H --> I[Evaporate to Dryness on Hot Plate]
    I --> J[Cool in Desiccator for 15 minutes]
    J --> K[Weigh Dried Residue to 0.0001 g precision]
    K --> L[Record m_t and elapsed t]
    L --> M{All Sampling Intervals Complete?}
    M -- No --> N[Wait Until Next Scheduled Time: 60, 180, 600, 1800, 7200, 21600 s]
    N --> E
    M -- Yes --> O[Compute Stokes Diameter D for Each t]
    O --> P[Compute Cumulative Percent Finer M equals m_t over m_0 times 100]
    P --> Q[Plot Cumulative Percent Finer vs D on semi-log paper]
    Q --> R[Plot Differential Frequency vs log D]
    R --> S[Report Median Size D50, Mode, and Distribution]
```

### 4.2 Working Schematic — Particle Selection by Sedimentation

The diagram below shows how the Andreasen technique selects particles of diameter $\le D$ at a given time $t$, by sampling only the *still-suspended* (smaller) particles from depth $h$:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph START_TIME ["t = 0: Homogeneous Suspension"]
        S1[Particle D1 large] --> M1[Settles Fast]
        S2[Particle D2 medium] --> M2[Settles Medium]
        S3[Particle D3 small] --> M3[Stays Suspended]
    end
    subgraph ELAPSED_TIME ["t later: Stratified Suspension"]
        M1 --> BELOW["BELOW h = 0.20 m: only D le Dmax"]
        M2 --> ATDEPTH["AT h = 0.20 m: D = Dmax boundary"]
        M3 --> ABOVE["ABOVE h = 0.20 m: D lt Dmax still in cloud"]
    end
    ABOVE --> PIPE[Andreasen Pipette Withdraws 10 cm3]
    ATDEPTH --> PIPE
    PIPE --> DRY[Oven Dry and Weigh]
    DRY --> CALC[Compute m_t, then D and M]
```

### 4.3 Typical Particle Size Distribution Curve (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PLOT ["Cumulative Percent Finer vs log D S-curve"]
        X["log D axis: 0.1 to 100 micrometres"] --> Y["Cumulative Percent Finer: 0 to 100"]
        P1[D10: 10 percent finer] --> P2[D50: median size 50 percent finer] --> P3[D90: 90 percent finer]
    end
```

> **Diagram Note:** The block diagrams above depict the **functional flow** of the experiment rather than a photorealistic schematic of the glassware, which is the recommended fallback per KTU V10 protocol for apparatus that cannot be cleanly rendered in Mermaid.
