## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
*State Stokes' law for terminal settling velocity of a particle in a fluid. List the assumptions made in its derivation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Stokes' law states that the terminal settling velocity of a spherical particle in a viscous fluid under laminar flow conditions is given by:

$$v_t = \frac{D^2 g (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \mu}$$

**Assumptions (must be listed for full marks):**
1. The particle is **rigid, smooth, and spherical**.
2. The fluid is **homogeneous, isotropic, and Newtonian**.
3. **Laminar flow** around the particle, i.e., particle Reynolds number $Re_p \le 0.2$.
4. The particle settles **without hindrance** from other particles (dilute suspension).
5. The vessel walls do not influence the flow (unbounded fluid).
6. **No slip** between fluid and particle surface.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest]**
*What is meant by "Stokes diameter" of a particle? Why is the Andreasen pipette method preferred over sieve analysis for fine powders below 75 μm?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Stokes diameter** of a particle is defined as the diameter of an *equivalent sphere* of the same density that has the **same terminal settling velocity** as the actual particle under the prevailing fluid conditions.

**Why Andreasen is preferred for sub-75 μm powders:**
1. Standard sieves with **woven wire mesh** have a practical lower limit of about **45 μm (325 mesh)**; particles below this size **clog the sieve openings** and cannot be reliably separated.
2. The Andreasen method extends the **measurable size range down to about 0.5–1 μm**.
3. It is **mechanically simple** and uses only gravimetric (weighing) measurements — no optical calibration needed.
4. It directly yields a **continuous distribution** at multiple sizes, not just two fractions as in sieving.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Examiner Note (KTU 2024 Module Internal Choice Format):**
> Answer **any ONE** of the following: (a) **Question A** OR (b) **Question B**. Each carries 14 marks (7 + 7).

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supple]**

**(a)** Derive the equation used in the Andreasen pipette method to calculate the **Stokes diameter** of a particle settling in a fluid. State clearly the assumptions involved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A suspension of calcium carbonate ($\rho_p = 2700 \ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$) in water at 30 °C ($\rho_f = 995.6 \ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$, $\mu = 0.798 \times 10^{-3} \ \mathrm{Pa \cdot s}$) is allowed to settle in an Andreasen pipette. The initial concentration corresponds to a dried mass of **0.600 g** per 10 cm³ of suspension. The pipette sampling depth is **$h = 0.20 \ \mathrm{m}$**. After a settling time of **$t = 900 \ \mathrm{s}$**, a 10 cm³ sample is withdrawn and the dried mass is found to be **$m = 0.300 \ \mathrm{g}$**. Calculate:
(i) The Stokes diameter $D$ at this time.
(ii) The cumulative mass percentage of particles finer than $D$.
(iii) Verify whether Stokes' law is applicable at this diameter. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:** (7 marks — full marks require all six sub-steps)

**Assumptions (1 mark):** Sphere, laminar flow, dilute suspension, no wall effects, Newtonian fluid, rigid smooth particle.

**Force balance (2 marks):**
$$F_g - F_b = F_d$$
$$\frac{\pi}{6} D^3 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g = 3 \pi \mu D v_t$$

**Simplification (1 mark):**
$$v_t = \frac{D^2 g (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \mu}$$

**Substitution $v_t = h/t$ (1 mark):**
$$v_t = \frac{h}{t} = \frac{D^2 g (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \mu}$$

**Final equation (1 mark):**
$$D = \sqrt{\frac{18 \mu h}{g (\rho_p - \rho_f) t}}$$

**Statement of applicability (1 mark):** $Re_p \le 0.2$.

**(b) Numerical Solution:** (7 marks)

**(i) Stokes diameter (3 marks):**

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{18 \times 0.798 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.20}{9.81 \times (2700 - 995.6) \times 900}}$$

Numerator: $18 \times 0.798 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.20 = 2.873 \times 10^{-3}$

Denominator: $9.81 \times 1704.4 \times 900 = 9.81 \times 1.5340 \times 10^{6} = 1.5049 \times 10^{7}$

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{2.873 \times 10^{-3}}{1.5049 \times 10^{7}}} = \sqrt{1.909 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$\boxed{D = 1.382 \times 10^{-5} \ \mathrm{m} = 13.82 \ \mathrm{\mu m}}$$

**(ii) Cumulative % Finer (2 marks):**

$$M(900) = \frac{0.300}{0.600} \times 100 = 50.0 \ \mathrm{\%}$$

**(iii) Reynolds number check (2 marks):**

$$v_t = \frac{0.20}{900} = 2.222 \times 10^{-4} \ \mathrm{m/s}$$

$$Re_p = \frac{1.382 \times 10^{-5} \times 2.222 \times 10^{-4} \times 995.6}{0.798 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$Re_p = \frac{3.058 \times 10^{-6}}{7.98 \times 10^{-4}} = 3.83 \times 10^{-3}$$

Since $Re_p = 0.00383 < 0.2$, **Stokes' law is valid**. ✓

---

#### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **experimental procedure** for the determination of particle size distribution of a fine powder using the Andreasen pipette. Include the **apparatus**, **chemicals/reagents**, and **graphical analysis** steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data were obtained in an Andreasen pipette experiment on a sample of silica ($\rho_p = 2650 \ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$) in water at 30 °C ($\rho_f = 1000 \ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$, $\mu = 0.801 \times 10^{-3} \ \mathrm{Pa \cdot s}$). Sampling depth $h = 0.20 \ \mathrm{m}$. Initial dry mass per 10 cm³ = 0.500 g. Calculate the **Stokes diameter** and **cumulative % finer** for each time, and plot the cumulative distribution curve. **(7 marks)**

| Time $t$ (s) | Dried mass $m$ (g) |
|:---:|:---:|
| 60 | 0.460 |
| 300 | 0.380 |
| 900 | 0.250 |
| 3600 | 0.140 |
| 10800 | 0.060 |
| 32400 | 0.020 |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Procedure (7 marks):**

**Apparatus (1 mark):** Andreasen pipette (graduated cylinder + side-sampling tube + 10 cm³ pipette with two-way stopcock), stopwatch, oven, desiccator, analytical balance (0.0001 g), 1000 cm³ beaker, hot plate, glass rod.

**Chemicals/Reagents (0.5 mark):** Sample powder, distilled water, dispersant (sodium hexametaphosphate or sodium silicate at 0.1–0.5 g/L to prevent flocculation).

**Procedure (4 marks):**
1. Weigh about **10 g of the dry, representative powder** sample (1 mark).
2. Prepare a **0.5–1.0 % w/v suspension** in 1000 cm³ of water containing the dispersant; stir thoroughly for 15 minutes to ensure complete dispersion (1 mark).
3. Transfer the suspension into the Andreasen cylinder; start the stopwatch immediately as $t = 0$. Maintain temperature at **30 °C** in a water bath (0.5 mark).
4. At predetermined time intervals (typically $t = 60, 300, 900, 3600, 10800, 32400 \ \mathrm{s}$), open the stopcock and **withdraw exactly 10 cm³** of suspension from the side sampling tube at $h = 0.20 \ \mathrm{m}$ (1 mark).
5. **Evaporate** each sample to dryness on a hot plate, **cool in a desiccator** for 15 minutes, and weigh to **0.0001 g** precision (0.5 mark).

**Graphical Analysis (1.5 marks):**
1. Calculate $D$ for each time using $D = \sqrt{18\mu h / [g(\rho_p - \rho_f) t]}$.
2. Calculate $M(t) = (m_t / m_0) \times 100$ for each time.
3. Plot **Cumulative % Finer vs $\log D$** (S-curve) on semi-log paper.
4. Read off **$D_{10}, D_{50}, D_{90}$** and identify the **mode** from the differential curve.

**(b) Numerical Calculations (7 marks):**

The precomputed denominator constant for this problem is:
$$g(\rho_p - \rho_f) = 9.81 \times 1650 = 1.6187 \times 10^{4}$$
$$18\mu h = 18 \times 0.801 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.20 = 2.8836 \times 10^{-3}$$

For each time, $D = \sqrt{2.8836 \times 10^{-3} / (1.6187 \times 10^{4} \times t)}$ μm (after unit conversion to μm):

| $t$ (s) | $m$ (g) | $D$ (μm) | $M(t) = (m/0.500) \times 100$ (%) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 60 | 0.460 | 54.5 | 92.0 |
| 300 | 0.380 | 24.4 | 76.0 |
| 900 | 0.250 | 14.1 | 50.0 |
| 3600 | 0.140 | 7.05 | 28.0 |
| 10800 | 0.060 | 4.07 | 12.0 |
| 32400 | 0.020 | 2.36 | 4.0 |

(1 mark for setup, 4 marks for computed values across 6 rows, 1 mark for $M(t)$ column, 1 mark for graph description.)

**Graph:** Plot of $M(t)$ vs $\log_{10} D$ — descending S-curve from 92% at 54.5 μm to 4% at 2.36 μm, median size $D_{50} \approx 14.1 \ \mathrm{\mu m}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the viscosity value at the experimental temperature.** Students often use $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s (water at 20 °C) instead of the correct value at 30 °C ($\mu \approx 0.798 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s). This **costs up to 2 marks** in calculations.
> 2. **Not verifying the Stokes' range** ($Re_p \le 0.2$) — KTU examiners *always* expect this check, even if not explicitly asked.
> 3. **Using $\rho_p - \rho_f$ incorrectly as $\rho_f$ alone or $\rho_p$ alone** — leads to wrong $D$ by orders of magnitude.
> 4. **Plotting diameter on a linear axis** instead of log scale — the cumulative curve will look distorted and the median reading will be wrong. **Always use semi-log paper.**
> 5. **Confusing the cumulative % finer with the cumulative % coarser** — read the question carefully: "particles finer than $D$" means undersize, "particles coarser than $D$" means oversize.
> 6. **Not mentioning the role of the dispersant** in the procedure — its function is to *deflocculate* the suspension, a frequently asked 1-mark sub-question.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Stokes' law** applies only when $Re_p \le 0.2$ (laminar flow).
- [x] The **Stokes diameter** is the diameter of an *equivalent sphere* having the *same terminal settling velocity*.
- [x] The **Andreasen pipette** measures particle size distribution in the range **~0.5 μm to 75 μm**.
- [x] Working equation: $D = \sqrt{18\mu h / [g(\rho_p - \rho_f) t]}$.
- [x] Sampling depth $h$ is **fixed** (0.20 m); sampling time $t$ is **variable**.
- [x] **Cumulative % finer** $M(t) = (m_t / m_0) \times 100$ where $m_0$ is the initial dry mass per sample volume.
- [x] Plot **Cumulative % Finer vs $\log D$** on semi-log paper.
- [x] A **dispersant** (e.g., sodium hexametaphosphate) is essential to prevent flocculation.
- [x] Suspension concentration must be **dilute** ($\le 1\%$ v/v) to avoid hindered settling.
- [x] **Stokes range** validity must be checked by computing $Re_p$ for the *smallest* and *largest* diameters in the range studied.
- [x] **Median size $D_{50}$** is the diameter at which cumulative % finer = 50%.
- [x] **Mode** is the diameter of maximum frequency on the differential distribution curve.
- [x] The **differential distribution** is plotted as $\Delta M / \Delta(\log D)$ vs $\log D$.
- [x] **Limitations:** assumes spherical particles, dilute suspension, Newtonian fluid, no wall effects.
- [x] **Standard $g$** = 9.81 m/s²; viscosity of water at 30 °C = $0.798 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy for this topic:**
> 1. Memorize the **working formula** and the **six assumptions** of Stokes' law.
> 2. Practice at least **two full numerical problems** — KTU usually gives one derivation (7 marks) and one calculation (7 marks) in Part B.
> 3. Always end the numerical part with a **Re-check** of the Stokes' range — this is a free 1 mark that 80% of students forget.
> 4. Be able to **sketch the S-curve** on semi-log paper from a given data table.
